content.text:
Backend Developer (Ruby on Rails) — Home Assignment
Overview
Build a small Rails app that reproduces a specific Jgive campaign donation page — the part a
donor sees and interacts with, up to (but not including) payment.

Your reference is this live campaign: https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-
targets/159183

Aim to get the result close to the existing design — it doesn't need to be pixel-perfect, but it
should clearly resemble the original. If something in the current design strikes you as off, feel
free to improve it — just tell us what you changed and why.
Time
Aim for roughly 4–6 hours. We care far more about your judgment than about completeness. If
you run short, cut scope deliberately and tell us what you cut and why. Please don't polish
endlessly — a focused, well-reasoned submission beats a sprawling one.
What to build (core)
A page for the campaign above that shows:
Campaign details, organized into tabs (mirroring the tabs on the reference page): title,
description / story, cover image, goal amount, amount raised, and progress toward the goal.
A donation form: amount (a few preset options + a custom amount), one-time vs. recurring,
donor display preference (full name / first name only / anonymous), and an optional
dedication message.
Submitting the form should create a donation record in a pending state and update the
campaign's progress. Do not implement payment / checkout. Instead, in your README, briefly
describe how you'd wire in a real payment provider and move a donation from pending to
paid .
Seed the app with one or two campaigns and a handful of donations so the page isn't empty on
first load.

Explicitly out of scope
Payment processing / checkout
User accounts, login, or admin
Production hardening

Deliverables
1. A public Git repo (or send us an invite). We'd love to see real commit history rather than a
single squashed commit.
2. A deployed, live application (mandatory). Deploy the app and include a link where we can
use it.
3. A README covering: how to run it locally, the key decisions and trade-offs you made, and
what you'd do with more time.
4. The full chat transcript(s) with whatever LLM / coding assistant you used.
5. A few sentences on your setup and thought process — your tools, how you approached
the problem, and where AI helped or got in your way.

On using AI
Use whatever tools you normally use. We use AI heavily here, and we want to see how you work
with it — not whether you avoided it. Sharing your transcript helps us understand how you steer,
review, and decide what to keep. You should also be ready to walk us through your code and
extend it together in the next interview.
Submitting
Send us the repo link, the live URL, and your transcript and notes. Questions are very welcome at
any point — just reach out.

Please output a structured step-by-step implementation plan optimized for speed and clean Rails conventions. 

Break the plan down into these phases:
Project Initialization & Configurations 
Database Schema & Setup (Migrations and Seeds)
Controller Logic & Routing
Frontend View Layout (Main page grid, JS/Hotwire Tabs, Sticky Form)
Deployment Strategy & README Outline

Do not write any application code yet. Just provide the step-by-step roadmap so we can align on the architecture first.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - API usage, tool use, Anthropic SDK usage. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

source "https://rubygems.org"

# Bundle edge Rails instead: gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
gem "rails", "~> 8.1.3"
# The modern asset pipeline for Rails [https://github.com/rails/propshaft]
gem "propshaft"
# Use sqlite3 as the database for Active Record
gem "sqlite3", ">= 2.1"
# Use the Puma web server [https://github.com/puma/puma]
gem "puma", ">= 5.0"
# Use JavaScript with ESM import maps [https://github.com/rails/importmap-rails]
gem "importmap-rails"
# Hotwire's SPA-like page accelerator [https://turbo.hotwired.dev]
gem "turbo-rails"
# Hotwire's modest JavaScript framework [https://stimulus.hotwired.dev]
gem "stimulus-rails"
# Build JSON APIs with ease [https://github.com/rails/jbuilder]
gem "jbuilder"

# Use Active Model has_secure_password [https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_model_basics.html#securepassword]
# gem "bcrypt", "~> 3.1.7"

# Windows does not include zoneinfo files, so bundle the tzinfo-data gem
gem "tzinfo-data", platforms: %i[ windows jruby ]

# Use the database-backed adapters for Rails.cache, Active Job, and Action Cable
gem "solid_cache"
gem "solid_queue"
gem "solid_cable"

# Reduces boot times through caching; required in config/boot.rb
gem "bootsnap", require: false

# Deploy this application anywhere as a Docker container [https://kamal-deploy.org]
gem "kamal", require: false

# Add HTTP asset caching/compression and X-Sendfile acceleration to Puma [https://github.com/basecamp/thruster/]
gem "thruster", require: false

# Use Active Storage variants [https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_storage_overview.html#transforming-images]
gem "image_processing", "~> 1.2"

group :development, :test do
  # See https://guides.rubyonrails.org/debugging_rails_applications.html#debugging-with-the-debug-gem
  gem "debug", platforms: %i[ mri windows ], require: "debug/prelude"

  # Audits gems for known security defects (use config/bundler-audit.yml to ignore issues)
  gem "bundler-audit", require: false

  # Static analysis for security vulnerabilities [https://brakemanscanner.org/]
  gem "brakeman", require: false

  # Omakase Ruby styling [https://github.com/rails/rubocop-rails-omakase/]
  gem "rubocop-rails-omakase", require: false
end

group :development do
  # Use console on exceptions pages [https://github.com/rails/web-console]
  gem "web-console"
end

group :test do
  # Use system testing [https://guides.rubyonrails.org/testing.html#system-testing]
  gem "capybara"
  gem "selenium-webdriver"
end

content.thinking:
Since this is a client-side Angular app with API-driven content, a standard curl request won't capture the rendered campaign structure—I'd need a headless browser for that. But given this is just planning work, I can leverage my familiarity with typical fundraising page patterns like JGive and Charidy to sketch out a solid approach. I could also try hunting for a JSON API endpoint the app calls to understand the underlying data model.

The standard campaign layout usually includes tabs for About/Story, Updates, and a donor list, alongside a donation widget with preset amounts, custom input, recurring options, donor name display, and dedication fields, plus cover imagery, a progress bar, and goal/raised metrics. Rather than spend too much time reverse-engineering the actual site, let me try one quick approach: search the HTML for embedded state objects like window.__INITIAL_STATE__ or check for likely API endpoint patterns, but I'll keep this brief since the goal is a plan, not exhaustive technical archaeology.

content.thinking:
I'm realizing I don't need to keep reverse-engineering their API when I already understand the standard fundraising campaign pattern well enough—title, hero image, progress bar showing raised versus goal, tabbed sections for About/Story/Updates/Comments, and a donation widget with preset amounts, custom entry, recurring options, and donor display settings. This aligns with what the assignment is asking for anyway.

Let me stop researching and move into planning. I should quickly verify the Rails 8 setup—checking the database config, Ruby version, and confirming the default stack (Solid Queue, Hotwire, importmap, SQLite)—then think through the architecture decisions, starting with whether to keep SQLite or switch to Postgres depending on the deployment target.

For styling, Tailwind via the tailwindcss-rails gem makes sense given the need for a polished card-based design with tabs and progress bars. On the database side, I'll go with decimal(10,2) for currency amounts in Israeli Shekels—simpler to read in seeds and views than storing as integer agorot, though I'll note the trade-off. Since the user asked me to align on architecture first before coding, I'll present these decisions as a structured plan rather than asking questions about each one.

The bigger decision that genuinely needs user input is the deployment target—Render, Fly.io, Heroku, or Railway—since it shapes both the database choice (SQLite vs Postgres) and all of Phase 5. I should ask about that since it's their call based on cost, familiarity, and existing accounts. I'm also uncertain about the current state of free-tier offerings across these platforms, so I'll need to flag that when I ask. I should ask the user about their deployment platform since that decision gates the database choice, asset storage approach, and other Phase 5 details. For the cover images, I'm leaning toward storing them as simple URL strings in a column rather than using Active Storage — since there's no admin upload UI, I can just seed campaigns with external image URLs or reference static asset files, which sidesteps the need for blob storage entirely and simplifies the whole setup.

Now I need to figure out the database strategy based on where they're deploying. SQLite works fine for Fly.io with persistent volumes, but Heroku's ephemeral filesystem would reset it daily, making Postgres necessary there. Render offers free Postgres which could work for the review timeline. Since my knowledge of current free tier details might be outdated, I should ask them directly which platform they prefer — that's a genuine decision point that affects the whole architecture.

I'll skip asking about CSS framework since Tailwind is a solid default recommendation that's easy to change later if needed. But deployment platform is worth clarifying now before I finalize the database choice for Phase 1.

On the donation logic: pending donations should count toward campaign progress since there's no actual payment processing in scope—the assignment explicitly says submitting the form updates progress, so I'll note this as a deliberate design choice for demo purposes while mentioning in the README that real-world systems would only count paid donations. For recurring donations, I just need a simple frequency flag (one-time vs monthly) with no actual billing logic. For donor display, I'll model it as an enum on the Donation record to handle full name, first name only, or anonymous preferences.

On the schema: I'm keeping the Donation model lean with just donor_name, display_preference, amount, frequency, and an optional dedication_message—skipping email since payment processing is out of scope and the spec doesn't request it. For campaigns, I need a Campaign model with title, description/story, cover image, goal amount, and a numeric ID for routing (skipping friendly_id gems to stay focused). The tabs should mirror typical donation site layouts with sections for about/description, story, and maybe a donors list, though I'm clarifying exactly which tabs the assignment requires. I'll note that I can't directly inspect the live reference page since it's a client-rendered app, so I'm basing the layout on the assignment description and standard crowdfunding conventions — I should verify the actual tab structure visually during the frontend phase, either by checking the site myself or asking the user for a screenshot.

For the schema, I'm thinking through the campaign model: a single `story` field for the narrative content (since the assignment treats "description/story" as one concept), plus a `summary` for the header and metadata. I'll keep it lean with title, summary, story, cover image URL, goal amount, and currency defaulting to ILS, avoiding unnecessary complexity like ActionText and just using plain text with simple formatting in the view.

For tracking raised amounts, I'm deciding against a denormalized counter column — instead I'll calculate it on-the-fly as a method that sums donations in valid states, which keeps the source of truth clean and avoids sync issues. This naturally satisfies the "update progress" requirement without extra migrations or maintenance overhead.

Now moving into the Donation model: it needs campaign reference, amount, frequency as an enum (one-time or monthly), display preference (full name, first name only, or anonymous), and donor name stored internally regardless of display setting.  I'm requiring the donor name always for receipts and records, but using display preference purely for what shows publicly — that's the actual meaning of "anonymous" here. Status will be pending or paid for now, with failed/cancelled noted as future enum values in the README when wiring a real payment provider.

For the controller layer, I need a campaigns index to list the seeded campaigns and a show page as the main focus, plus a nested donations create action under each campaign that redirects back with a success message.  I'll use Turbo Stream on donation creation to update the progress bar, raised amount, and recent donors list in place without a full reload, which aligns nicely with the Hotwire emphasis.

The frontend layout is a two-column grid with the campaign cover, title, and tabs (About/Story, Donors) on the left, and a sticky donation form card on the right that stacks on mobile. I'm building a Stimulus controller for the tabs to handle show/hide, aria attributes, and URL hash syncing.

For the progress bar, I'll create a partial that displays the raised amount against the goal with a percentage-width div. The sticky form uses CSS positioning with optional Stimulus for mobile collapse behavior, though CSS alone should be sufficient. I'm using tailwindcss-rails to style everything with utility classes matching the reference design.

For deployment and documentation, I'll outline the setup steps, explain the key architectural decisions like the derived raised_amount field and pending donations counting toward progress, and note what I'd prioritize with more time—real payment integration, friendly_id slugs, recurring billing via webhooks, an admin panel, image uploads, expanded test coverage, and RTL/Hebrew i18n support since the reference site uses those.

On payment integration, I'd describe the flow: create a pending Donation, redirect to the provider's checkout session (Stripe, Tranzila, or PayMe for Israeli processors), handle the webhook callback with an idempotency key to mark the donation as paid, then redirect back to the campaign with a thank-you message.

There's a notable decision here about language and layout direction—the reference site is Hebrew with RTL layout, but building that would add significant scope. I should surface this trade-off to the user now rather than silently choosing English/LTR for speed, since it affects how closely the result matches the reference design.

I'll ask two focused questions together: deployment platform (Render, Fly.io, Heroku, or other) and whether to build in Hebrew/RTL or English/LTR for development efficiency.

questions.question: Where do you want to deploy the live app? This decides the production DB (SQLite vs Postgres) and what Phase 5 actually contains.

options.label: Render (Recommended)
options.description: Builds straight from the Dockerfile already in the repo. Pairs well with keeping SQLite on a small persistent disk (simplest), or you can attach Render's managed Postgres instead.

options.label: Fly.io
options.description: Designed around SQLite + persistent volumes (matches Rails 8's default stance), but needs flyctl installed and a card on file even for the hobby allowance.

options.label: Heroku
options.description: Most familiar workflow, but its filesystem is ephemeral — SQLite would get wiped on every dyno restart, so we'd switch to Heroku Postgres.

options.label: Not sure / you decide
options.description: I'll default to Render + SQLite with a small persistent disk — least moving parts for a 4-6hr assignment.

questions.question: The live reference page is Hebrew/RTL. How closely should we mirror that vs. building in English for speed?

options.label: English / LTR (Recommended)
options.description: Mirror the same layout, sections, tabs and sticky form structurally, but write copy in English and keep standard LTR CSS. Fastest — no i18n/RTL work.

options.label: Hebrew / RTL to match
options.description: Set dir="rtl", add Hebrew copy via Rails i18n, and use RTL-aware CSS. Closer visual match to the source but adds real time to the frontend phase.

lastPrompt: Backend Developer (Ruby on Rails) — Home Assignment Overview Build a small Rails app that reproduces a specific Jgive campaign donation page — the part a donor sees and interacts with, up to (but not…

content.thinking:
I've settled on Render with SQLite and persistent disk storage, plus English/LTR support. Now I'm mapping out the full implementation roadmap across the five phases—listing the gems I'll need to add, the database migrations and columns, the routes and controller actions, the views and partials, any Stimulus controllers, and the deployment steps specific to Render. I'm also pinning down some specifics like using `tailwindcss-rails` for styling.

Since Rails 8 already uses Propshaft and importmap without Node, I'll stick with the `tailwindcss-rails` gem which bundles a precompiled Tailwind CLI—keeps everything Node-free and simple. For the models, I'm going with Campaign and Donation, using Rails 8's native `enum` for fields like frequency, display_preference, and status. Validations are straightforward: Campaign checks title and goal_amount > 0, Donation checks amount > 0 and donor_name presence. For the progress calculation, I'm creating instance methods on Campaign for `raised_amount` (summing donations), `progress_percentage` (clamped 0–100), and `donations_count`. Since the assignment wants progress to update on form submission, I'll count both pending and paid donations toward the raised total by default, but I'll isolate this logic in a named scope `Donation.countable` so it's easy to flip to "only paid" later once real payment processing is integrated.

The Donors tab pulls from `campaign.donations.countable.order(created_at: :desc)` and respects the display_preference enum—showing full name, first name only, or "Anonymous" accordingly. Routes are nested: campaigns index/show with donations create nested underneath, plus a root route to campaigns#index. The DonationsController#create action builds the donation via `campaign.donations.build`, saves it, and responds with a turbo_stream update to refresh the progress bar, donor list, and flash message; on HTML requests it redirects back to the campaign with a notice. If validation fails, I'll re-render the show template with errors and accept that the tab state resets to default, which is acceptable for this scope. The seed file populates 2 campaigns at different progress levels with 4–8 donations spread across them, varying frequency and display preferences to showcase the donors tab and progress bar nicely.

For Phase 5 deployment on Render with SQLite and persistent disk, the Rails 8 default Dockerfile already includes database setup via `db:prepare` in the entrypoint, so I need to verify that's in place and create a render.yaml configuration file. Verify .ruby-version aligns with the local setup and Render's available Ruby images. For gems, I'll skip extra dependencies like `annotate` or `factory_bot_rails`—instead, I'll use hand-written seed data for clarity and determinism rather than Faker, keeping the dataset explicit and easy to review. Action Cable and Solid Cable aren't needed since Turbo Streams work over standard HTTP, so I'll leave those Rails 8 defaults as harmless unused cruft. The app is already on Rails 8.1.3, so no need to run `bundle install` or `app:update`—I just need to finalize the database configuration.

For production, Rails 8's default SQLite setup already points to `/rails/storage/production.sqlite3`, which aligns perfectly with Render's persistent disk mount, so no changes needed there. I'll defer tailwind color customization until Phase 4 when I can reference actual branding. I should run `bin/rubocop -A` as a Phase 1 baseline cleanup step, and establish a practice of making small, logical commits per phase to build a real commit history throughout the project.

Now moving into Phase 2, I'm generating the Campaign model with fields for title, summary, story, cover image URL, goal amount, and a slug for routing.

Actually, looking at the reference URL pattern—it uses numeric IDs like 159183, not slugs—so I'll drop the slug field and stick with simple numeric ID routes. That's more authentic to the original design. For the goal amount, I'll use decimal with precision 10 and scale 2, specified in the migration rather than the generator shorthand.

Next is the Donation model with campaign references, amount, frequency, status, and display preference fields. I'll set defaults at the database level: frequency defaults to 0 (one-time), status to 0 (pending), and display preference to 0 (full name). The campaign_id index comes automatically with references, and I'll skip additional status indexing since it's not needed at this scale.

For the model layer, Campaign has many donations with dependent destroy, Donation uses enums for its integer fields, and I'll add validations plus helper methods like raised_amount, progress_percentage, and donations_count. Then in seeds, I'll create two realistic campaigns with Hebrew-appropriate generic names and goal amounts around ₪50,000 and ₪20,000, each with a short summary and multi-paragraph story text.

For images, I'm bundling local placeholder images directly in app/assets/images/seeds/ and storing just the filename in the database, then rendering them with image_tag in views—this keeps everything self-contained without external dependencies or Active Storage complexity. Then I'll seed 6-10 donations across both campaigns with varied amounts, frequencies, display preferences, and statuses (mostly pending with maybe one or two marked paid), some with dedication messages and some blank. I'll use the find_or_create_by pattern for idempotent reseeding.

For Phase 3, the routes set up a root to campaigns index with nested donation creation under each campaign, and the CampaignsController handles index and show actions, building a new donation instance for the form.

The DonationsController create action finds the campaign, builds and saves the donation, then responds with either a turbo stream that updates the progress bar, prepends the new donor to the list (with anonymous logic handled), and resets the form with a success message, or falls back to an HTML redirect with a thank you notice. On validation errors, it re-renders the form partial with errors via turbo stream or re-renders the campaign show page for HTML requests. Strong params permit amount, frequency, display_preference, donor_name, and dedication_message, with custom amounts handled entirely client-side so the controller stays clean. I'm also noting minimal test stubs—model validation tests and one happy-path donation creation request test—as a time-permitting addition, otherwise deferred to the README.

For Phase 4, I'm starting with the layout shell...

Building the campaign pages...

The donation form sits sticky in the right column, collecting amount via preset buttons plus an optional custom input field (a Stimulus controller toggles which one is active), frequency as a toggle between one-time and monthly, display preference as radio options, donor name, and optional dedication message — validation errors display above the form if needed. Wrapping everything in Turbo Frames so the progress bar, donor list, and form update together after submission.

For styling, I'm applying Tailwind utilities for shadows, rounded corners, brand colors, and responsive breakpoints so the form stacks below the tabs on mobile and stays sticky on desktop. The two Stimulus controllers (tabs and amount sync) are lightweight with no build complications since we're using importmap.

For deployment, I need to verify the Dockerfile runs migrations and seeds on startup, check the entrypoint script, confirm the production database path, and add a render.yaml file describing the web service configuration.

Then I'll push to GitHub, connect Render to the repo, manually set the RAILS_MASTER_KEY secret in the dashboard, and trigger the deploy. After the first successful build, I'll run the seed data via Render's shell, smoke-test the live URL across desktop and mobile, and clean up any unused Kamal config files. The README will document the live URL, local setup steps, and the architecture decisions made throughout the project.

For the architecture section, I'm noting that I derived the raised amount instead of storing a denormalized counter, made pending donations count toward progress per the assignment requirements using a `Donation.countable` scope for easy future changes, bundled cover images as local assets since there's no upload UI in scope, used Tailwind via the existing importmap setup to avoid Node dependencies, and chose SQLite with Render's persistent disk over Postgres to keep infrastructure minimal. I'm also documenting how I'd integrate a real payment provider—creating donations as pending, redirecting to a checkout flow with the donation ID in metadata, then flipping the status to paid only after a verified webhook to ensure idempotency and handle failures gracefully. Finally, I'm listing what I'd tackle with more time: comprehensive tests, Hebrew/RTL support with i18n, Active Storage for image uploads with a basic admin interface, friendly URL slugs, and recurring billing automation.

I should also add a placeholder section documenting any design improvements I make during Phase 4, include notes about AI usage with links to chat transcripts per the deliverables, and commit after each phase with descriptive messages to build proper history. Before wrapping up, I want to verify that the production database config actually uses the persistent storage path so my deployment claims are accurate rather than assumed.

file.content:
# SQLite. Versions 3.8.0 and up are supported.
#   gem install sqlite3
#
#   Ensure the SQLite 3 gem is defined in your Gemfile
#   gem "sqlite3"
#
default: &default
  adapter: sqlite3
  max_connections: <%= ENV.fetch("RAILS_MAX_THREADS") { 5 } %>
  timeout: 5000

development:
  <<: *default
  database: storage/development.sqlite3

# Warning: The database defined as "test" will be erased and
# re-generated from your development database when you run "rake".
# Do not set this db to the same as development or production.
test:
  <<: *default
  database: storage/test.sqlite3

# Store production database in the storage/ directory, which by default
# is mounted as a persistent Docker volume in config/deploy.yml.
production:
  primary:
    <<: *default
    database: storage/production.sqlite3
  cache:
    <<: *default
    database: storage/production_cache.sqlite3
    migrations_paths: db/cache_migrate
  queue:
    <<: *default
    database: storage/production_queue.sqlite3
    migrations_paths: db/queue_migrate
  cable:
    <<: *default
    database: storage/production_cable.sqlite3
    migrations_paths: db/cable_migrate

52	RUN bundle exec bootsnap precompile -j 1 app/ lib/

file.content:
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1
# check=error=true

# This Dockerfile is designed for production, not development. Use with Kamal or build'n'run by hand:
# docker build -t jgive_assignment .
# docker run -d -p 80:80 -e RAILS_MASTER_KEY=<value from config/master.key> --name jgive_assignment jgive_assignment

# For a containerized dev environment, see Dev Containers: https://guides.rubyonrails.org/getting_started_with_devcontainer.html

# Make sure RUBY_VERSION matches the Ruby version in .ruby-version
ARG RUBY_VERSION=3.3.1
FROM docker.io/library/ruby:$RUBY_VERSION-slim AS base

# Rails app lives here
WORKDIR /rails

# Install base packages
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y curl libjemalloc2 libvips sqlite3 && \
    ln -s /usr/lib/$(uname -m)-linux-gnu/libjemalloc.so.2 /usr/local/lib/libjemalloc.so && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Set production environment variables and enable jemalloc for reduced memory usage and latency.
ENV RAILS_ENV="production" \
    BUNDLE_DEPLOYMENT="1" \
    BUNDLE_PATH="/usr/local/bundle" \
    BUNDLE_WITHOUT="development" \
    LD_PRELOAD="/usr/local/lib/libjemalloc.so"

# Throw-away build stage to reduce size of final image
FROM base AS build

# Install packages needed to build gems
RUN apt-get update -qq && \
    apt-get install --no-install-recommends -y build-essential git libvips libyaml-dev pkg-config && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists /var/cache/apt/archives

# Install application gems
COPY vendor/* ./vendor/
COPY Gemfile Gemfile.lock ./

RUN bundle install && \
    rm -rf ~/.bundle/ "${BUNDLE_PATH}"/ruby/*/cache "${BUNDLE_PATH}"/ruby/*/bundler/gems/*/.git && \
    # -j 1 disable parallel compilation to avoid a QEMU bug: https://github.com/rails/bootsnap/issues/495
    bundle exec bootsnap precompile -j 1 --gemfile

# Copy application code
COPY . .

# Precompile bootsnap code for faster boot times.
# -j 1 disable parallel compilation to avoid a QEMU bug: https://github.com/rails/bootsnap/issues/495
RUN bundle exec bootsnap precompile -j 1 app/ lib/

# Precompiling assets for production without requiring secret RAILS_MASTER_KEY
RUN SECRET_KEY_BASE_DUMMY=1 ./bin/rails assets:precompile




# Final stage for app image
FROM base

# Run and own only the runtime files as a non-root user for security
RUN groupadd --system --gid 1000 rails && \
    useradd rails --uid 1000 --gid 1000 --create-home --shell /bin/bash
USER 1000:1000

# Copy built artifacts: gems, application
COPY --chown=rails:rails --from=build "${BUNDLE_PATH}" "${BUNDLE_PATH}"
COPY --chown=rails:rails --from=build /rails /rails

# Entrypoint prepares the database.
ENTRYPOINT ["/rails/bin/docker-entrypoint"]

# Start server via Thruster by default, this can be overwritten at runtime
EXPOSE 80
CMD ["./bin/thrust", "./bin/rails", "server"]

lastPrompt: Backend Developer (Ruby on Rails) — Home Assignment Overview Build a small Rails app that reproduces a specific Jgive campaign donation page — the part a donor sees and interacts with, up to (but not…

content.thinking:
The database configuration already points to a persistent SQLite file that's mounted as a Docker volume, which means the same setup can work on Render by mounting a persistent disk at `/rails/storage` instead. The Dockerfile's entrypoint handles database preparation, so my Phase 5 approach of mounting the disk and letting the existing setup handle the rest should work.

Now I'm ready to write out the full five-phase plan as a structured response, keeping it organized with clear headers and bullets while noting the two key decisions (Render + SQLite, English/LTR) upfront.

content.text:
Two decisions locked in: **Render + SQLite w/ persistent disk**, and **English/LTR** (structurally mirroring the reference's sections/tabs/sticky-form, not its language). Plan below reflects both. No code yet — this is the roadmap to align on.

---

## Phase 1 — Project Initialization & Configuration

1. **Keep the Rails 8.1 skeleton as-is** — Propshaft, importmap, Turbo/Stimulus, SQLite, Solid Queue/Cache/Cable, Kamal/Thruster scaffolding are already in `Gemfile`. No Rails upgrade needed.
2. **Add one gem: `tailwindcss-rails`** for styling speed/fidelity. It ships a standalone Tailwind binary, so it stays Node-free and fits the existing importmap setup — no `package.json`, no JS build step.
3. **Deliberately skip:** Active Storage, ActionText, `friendly_id`, Faker/FactoryBot. Rationale for each lands in Phase 2/4 below — flagging now so it's clear these are intentional omissions, not oversights.
4. **Cosmetic cleanup:** `.kamal/` and `config/deploy.yml` are dead weight once we deploy via Render's native Dockerfile build instead of Kamal — either delete them or leave a one-line README note so reviewers aren't confused about the actual deploy path.
5. `bundle install`, run `bin/rubocop -A` once to confirm a clean omakase baseline before any app code lands.
6. Establish the commit cadence now: one commit per phase/sub-step (not a squash at the end), since "real commit history" is an explicit deliverable.

## Phase 2 — Database Schema & Setup (Migrations + Seeds)

**`Campaign`**
- `title:string`, `summary:string` (one-liner for cards/header), `story:text` (long-form narrative, rendered with `simple_format` — no ActionText, since there's no rich-text upload UI to justify it), `cover_image_url:string`, `goal_amount:decimal, precision: 10, scale: 2`.
- No `slug` — numeric ID routes (`/campaigns/159183`-style) actually match the reference URL's own pattern, so this is free authenticity, not a shortcut.
- **No raised-amount column.** `raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` / `donations_count` are instance methods derived from the `donations` association, not a denormalized counter — at this scale (a handful of seed donations, single process) deriving from the source of truth is simpler and can't drift out of sync. Worth a one-line callout in the README as a deliberate trade-off (would reconsider with real traffic).

**`Donation`**
- `campaign:references`, `amount:decimal, precision: 10, scale: 2`, `frequency:integer` enum (`one_time`/`monthly`, default `one_time`), `display_preference:integer` enum (`full_name`/`first_name_only`/`anonymous`, default `full_name`), `status:integer` enum (`pending`/`paid`, default `pending`), `donor_name:string`, `dedication_message:text` (optional).
- `donor_name` is **always required**, even when `display_preference: anonymous` — anonymity controls what's shown publicly, not what's collected internally. Worth stating explicitly since it's a subtle distinction.
- A `Donation.countable` scope (`pending` + `paid`) is what `Campaign#raised_amount` sums. The assignment explicitly wants progress to move when a *pending* donation is created (since payment is out of scope), so pending counts for now — but isolating that policy in one named scope means switching to "only `paid` counts" later (once a real payment provider lands) is a one-line change, not a hunt through the codebase.

**Seeds (`db/seeds.rb`)**
- 2 campaigns, hand-written (not Faker) — deterministic, readable in a diff, no extra dependency for ~2 records.
- Cover images: 1-2 image files checked into `app/assets/images/seeds/`, referenced by filename and rendered via `image_tag`. No Active Storage, no external hotlinked URL — keeps the demo fully self-contained and immune to a dead link during review.
- 6-10 donations spread across both campaigns, varied `frequency`/`display_preference`/`dedication_message` (some blank), mostly `pending` with one or two `paid` to prove the model supports it. Idempotent (`find_or_create_by!` / clear-and-reseed) so it's safe to rerun in production.

## Phase 3 — Controller Logic & Routing

```
root to: "campaigns#index"
resources :campaigns, only: [:index, :show] do
  resources :donations, only: [:create]
end
```

- **`CampaignsController#index`** — minimal card grid, not the graded focus.
- **`CampaignsController#show`** — loads `@campaign`, a new unsaved `@donation` for the form, and `@campaign.donations.countable.recent` for the Donors tab.
- **`DonationsController#create`** — builds under `@campaign`, strong-params on `:amount, :frequency, :display_preference, :donor_name, :dedication_message`. Responds to:
  - `turbo_stream`: replace the progress bar, prepend to the donor list, swap the form for a thank-you state (or re-render the form with errors, `422`, on failure).
  - `html`: redirect to the campaign with a flash notice — standard Turbo fallback, no extra work needed.
- Custom-amount handling stays server-naive: the "preset vs. custom" merge is a client-side JS concern (Phase 4) so the controller only ever sees one `donation[amount]` value.
- If time allows: a model validation test + one request spec for donation creation. If cut, it's named explicitly in the README rather than silently dropped.

## Phase 4 — Frontend (Grid, Hotwire Tabs, Sticky Form)

- **Layout:** light header, `<main>` yield, Tailwind loaded via `tailwindcss-rails`.
- **Show page structure:**
  - Hero: cover image, title, summary.
  - Stats bar: raised / goal, a reusable `_progress_bar` partial (shared with index cards), donor count.
  - Responsive grid below (`grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-3`): left 2 cols = tabs, right 1 col = donation form.
- **Tabs** ("About", "Story", "Donors") — a small Stimulus `tabs_controller.js` toggling a `hidden` class + `aria-selected` on server-rendered panels. No Turbo Frames: content's already in the DOM, so this is pure client-side show/hide — fastest option, no extra round-trip.
- **Sticky form** — plain CSS `position: sticky; top: …` on the right-column wrapper; no JS required.
- **Donation form:**
  - Amount: preset radio buttons + a separate "other amount" input. A tiny Stimulus `amount_controller.js` makes typing into "other" activate it as the selected radio, so exactly one `donation[amount]` is ever submitted — no server-side merge logic.
  - Frequency toggle (one-time/monthly), display-preference radios, donor name, optional dedication textarea, submit.
- **Checkpoint before calling this phase done:** pull up the live reference page and eyeball spacing/colors/section order against ours — I couldn't scrape its actual markup (it's a client-rendered SPA that 404s on guessed API paths), so the tab names/order above are inferred from the assignment brief + typical crowdfunding-page conventions, not a literal copy. Any visual deltas worth calling out go straight into the README's "what I changed and why" section per the brief's own invitation.

## Phase 5 — Deployment Strategy & README

**Deployment (Render, Docker-native, SQLite + persistent disk)**
1. Confirm `bin/docker-entrypoint` runs `db:prepare` on boot and `config/database.yml`'s production path (`storage/production.sqlite3`) lines up with a disk mount at `/rails/storage`.
2. Add a `render.yaml` blueprint: web service, `env: docker`, free plan, one disk mounted at `/rails/storage` (1GB), `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` as a dashboard secret, `RAILS_ENV=production`. Gives reviewers a reproducible deploy config, not just a dashboard click-through.
3. Push to a public GitHub repo, connect Render (Blueprint or manual Web Service), set the master key secret, deploy.
4. Run `bin/rails db:seed` once via Render's shell after first deploy (seeds are idempotent, safe to rerun on redeploys).
5. Smoke-test the live URL: index → show → tab switching → submit a donation → progress bar and donor list update.

**README outline**
- Live URL + repo link.
- Run locally: `bin/setup`, `bin/rails db:seed`, `bin/dev`.
- Key decisions & trade-offs: derived `raised_amount` vs. counter cache; pending donations counting toward progress (explicit `Donation.countable` scope as the seam for later); no Active Storage/admin (bundled seed images, no upload UI in scope); Tailwind for Node-free speed; SQLite+disk over Postgres at this scale; English/LTR for time-budget reasons, structural-not-literal mirroring of the reference.
- **Wiring a real payment provider:** Donation created `pending` → redirect to a provider-hosted checkout (Stripe Checkout, or an Israeli processor like Tranzila/PayMe — jgive itself loads `cdn.payme.io`) with the donation id in metadata → webhook verifies signature, flips status to `paid`, stores the provider's transaction id under a unique index for idempotency → failure/cancel webhook handled separately → redirect user back to a thank-you state. Only `paid` would count toward `Donation.countable` at that point.
- What I'd do with more time: test coverage, Hebrew/RTL + i18n, real image uploads + a minimal admin, recurring-billing automation, donor receipts, accessibility pass.
- AI usage note + pointer to the transcript file(s).

---

Want me to adjust anything here before I start on Phase 1?

I choose Render with SQLite and a small persistent disk. Please write the configuration files (like render.yaml or Dockerfile if needed) and the plan according to this deployment target.
create  a md file of this plan

content.thinking: The user is confirming they want to deploy on Render with SQLite and a persistent disk, so I need to create the render.yaml configuration file and a markdown plan document tailored to that setup. Checking the Docker entrypoint and Thruster configuration to ensure the app listens on the PORT environment variable that Render injects, since Thruster's HTTP_PORT and TARGET_PORT settings need to align with Render's expectations for the container to bind correctly. Since Thruster defaults to port 80 and the Dockerfile already exposes it, I'll leave the configuration as-is and just document in the render.yaml that it uses the existing port setup. Now I need to verify that the docker-entrypoint script handles database preparation automatically and check for any Rails master key requirements that might cause startup failures.

lastPrompt: I choose Render with SQLite and a small persistent disk. Please write the configuration files (like render.yaml or Dockerfile if needed) and the plan according to this deployment target. create  a md…

content.thinking:
Confirmed that `db:prepare` runs on container startup before the Rails server, so seeds won't execute automatically—I'll need to run `bin/rails db:seed` manually through the Render shell after the first deploy. Ruby version 3.3.1 matches the Dockerfile, so that's consistent. Now I'm moving on to write the render.yaml configuration.

Wait—I need to flag something critical: Render's free tier doesn't actually support persistent disks. Disks require at least the Starter paid plan ($7/month), so if the user wants SQLite with persistent storage, they'll need to upgrade from free. Let me note this clearly rather than silently writing a config that won't work.

The real issue is that without a disk on the free tier, SQLite data lives in ephemeral storage and gets wiped whenever the container restarts—which happens after 15 minutes of inactivity on Render's free tier. That means any donations submitted during grading could vanish on the next spin-down, and even seeded data would disappear unless it's reseeded on every restart. This breaks the assignment requirement that the page shouldn't be empty on first load and makes grading unreliable. I should present this as a choice: either pay for Starter to get a persistent disk, or accept the limitations of ephemeral storage on free tier. I could auto-seed the database on every boot to work around the free tier's lack of persistent storage — the app would always start with campaign data populated, satisfying the functional requirements within a single review session, even though donations wouldn't survive across cold starts. That's actually a clean solution for a take-home assignment where persistence across days isn't a stated requirement. But you explicitly asked for a persistent disk, so I should flag this constraint directly: Render's free tier doesn't support disks at all, only paid plans do. This is a real cost decision — either upgrade to their Starter plan (~$7/month) or accept the ephemeral storage workaround. I need to ask which direction you want to go before I write the config files, since it changes what's actually deployable. I should verify Render's current free tier policy with a quick check of their documentation to make sure my recommendation is grounded in what's actually available today rather than relying on potentially outdated knowledge.

content.text: Before writing the config, I want to verify one thing that affects which files are even correct to write.

input.prompt: Does Render's free instance type support persistent disks? What is the minimum paid plan required to attach a persistent disk to a web service? What happens to a free-tier web service's filesystem on restart/spin-down?

content.content:
# Render Persistent Disks: Free vs. Paid Tiers

Based on the documentation provided:

**Free instances cannot use persistent disks.** The page explicitly states you can attach persistent disks to "a paid Render web service, private service, or background worker."

**Minimum requirement:** You need a **paid plan** to attach a persistent disk to any web service.

**Free-tier filesystem behavior:** The documentation notes that "by default, Render services have an ephemeral filesystem." This means free instances lose "any changes you make to a service's local files...every time the service redeploys or restarts."

In essence, free-tier services operate with temporary storage only, while persistent disk functionality is exclusively a paid feature.

# Render Persistent Disks: Free vs. Paid Tiers

Based on the documentation provided:

**Free instances cannot use persistent disks.** The page explicitly states you can attach persistent disks to "a paid Render web service, private service, or background worker."

**Minimum requirement:** You need a **paid plan** to attach a persistent disk to any web service.

**Free-tier filesystem behavior:** The documentation notes that "by default, Render services have an ephemeral filesystem." This means free instances lose "any changes you make to a service's local files...every time the service redeploys or restarts."

In essence, free-tier services operate with temporary storage only, while persistent disk functionality is exclusively a paid feature.

questions.question: Render's free tier can't attach a persistent disk (confirmed in their docs just now). How do you want to handle this?

options.label: Free tier + auto-reseed on boot (Recommended)
options.description: $0, no card on file. Add a boot-time check that reseeds campaigns/donations if the table is empty, so the page is never blank after a cold start. Trade-off: any donation submitted during a session is lost once the free service spins down (after ~15min idle) and restarts — acceptable since cross-session persistence isn't a stated requirement.

options.label: Paid Starter instance + real persistent disk
options.description: Matches what you originally asked for — SQLite data actually survives restarts/redeploys. Requires a credit card on the Render account and a small recurring cost (check current pricing in the Render dashboard).

content.thinking:
I've settled on the free tier with auto-reseeding on boot, so no persistent disk is needed—ephemeral storage works fine since we'll reseed every time the service starts. Now I'm mapping out the render.yaml configuration and any necessary Dockerfile or entrypoint adjustments. I'll set up the web service with Docker runtime on the free plan without a disk block, configure environment variables like RAILS_MASTER_KEY (manually set, not synced) and RAILS_ENV=production, and add a health check.

For the health check endpoint, Rails 7.1+ automatically includes a `/up` route that points to the built-in health check controller, so I should verify that's present in the routes file. Then I need to modify the docker-entrypoint script to handle auto-seeding: after running `db:prepare`, I'll add a step to invoke `db:seed` on every boot. Since we're using ephemeral storage, the SQLite file gets wiped fresh each time anyway, so running the seed unconditionally is simpler than adding a conditional check—as long as the seed file uses idempotent operations like `find_or_create_by!`.

  # Define your application routes per the DSL in https://guides.rubyonrails.org/routing.html

  # Reveal health status on /up that returns 200 if the app boots with no exceptions, otherwise 500.
  # Can be used by load balancers and uptime monitors to verify that the app is live.
  get "up" => "rails/health#show", as: :rails_health_check

  # Defines the root path route ("/")

end

For this phase, I need to focus on the configuration files and documentation. I'm going to create the render.yaml deployment config, update the docker-entrypoint script to automatically reseed the database on boot since we're working with ephemeral storage, and write out the implementation plan as a markdown file in the repo root.

The Dockerfile doesn't need any changes since it already copies the entrypoint script and sets it correctly, and Render should auto-detect the EXPOSE 80 port. Now I'm working on the render.yaml file using Render's Blueprint schema — I need to verify whether to use `runtime: docker` or the older `env: docker` key, along with getting the exact field names for plan type, health check paths, and environment variables to ensure the config is accurate.

input.prompt: Give me the exact YAML keys for a render.yaml Blueprint that defines a single Docker-runtime web service: the top-level structure, the key for runtime (runtime vs env), the key for the plan/instance type (and the exact free-tier value), dockerfilePath, dockerContext, healthCheckPath, and the envVars list syntax including how to mark a secret as set manually in the dashboard (sync: false). Quote the keys exactly as documented.

content.content:
# Render.yaml Blueprint Keys for Docker Web Service

Based on the documentation, here are the exact YAML keys:

**Top-level structure:**
- `"services"` - list containing service objects

**Runtime specification:**
- `"runtime"` - use value `"docker"` for Docker-based services (not `env`)

**Instance type:**
- `"plan"` - use `"free"` for the free tier value

**Docker-specific keys:**
- `"dockerfilePath"` - path to Dockerfile relative to repo root
- `"dockerContext"` - Docker build context path relative to repo root

**Health checks:**
- `"healthCheckPath"` - "The path of the service's health check endpoint"

**Environment variables:**
- `"envVars"` - list of environment variable objects
- Within each variable object, set `"sync: false"` to prompt for manual dashboard input during initial setup

The documentation notes that `"sync: false"` variables are "prompted you for these values _only during the initial Blueprint creation flow_" and are ignored when updating existing Blueprints.

# Render Blueprint — see https://render.com/docs/blueprint-spec
#
# Deploys straight from the repo's existing Dockerfile. No persistent disk:
# the free instance type's filesystem is ephemeral by design (Render docs:
# disks require a paid plan), so SQLite resets on every restart/redeploy.
# bin/docker-entrypoint runs db:prepare + db:seed on every boot to make that
# a non-issue — the app always comes up populated, never blank.
services:
  - type: web
    name: jgive-assignment
    runtime: docker
    plan: free
    dockerfilePath: ./Dockerfile
    dockerContext: .
    healthCheckPath: /up
    envVars:
      - key: RAILS_MASTER_KEY
        sync: false # paste the contents of config/master.key in the Render dashboard
      - key: RAILS_ENV
        value: production
      - key: RAILS_LOG_TO_STDOUT
        value: "true"

require "active_support/core_ext/integer/time"

Rails.application.configure do
  # Settings specified here will take precedence over those in config/application.rb.

  # Code is not reloaded between requests.
  config.enable_reloading = false

  # Eager load code on boot for better performance and memory savings (ignored by Rake tasks).
  config.eager_load = true

  # Full error reports are disabled.
  config.consider_all_requests_local = false

  # Turn on fragment caching in view templates.
  config.action_controller.perform_caching = true

  # Cache assets for far-future expiry since they are all digest stamped.
  config.public_file_server.headers = { "cache-control" => "public, max-age=#{1.year.to_i}" }

  # Enable serving of images, stylesheets, and JavaScripts from an asset server.
  # config.asset_host = "http://assets.example.com"

  # Store uploaded files on the local file system (see config/storage.yml for options).
  config.active_storage.service = :local

  # Assume all access to the app is happening through a SSL-terminating reverse proxy.
  # config.assume_ssl = true

  # Force all access to the app over SSL, use Strict-Transport-Security, and use secure cookies.
  # config.force_ssl = true

  # Skip http-to-https redirect for the default health check endpoint.
  # config.ssl_options = { redirect: { exclude: ->(request) { request.path == "/up" } } }

  # Log to STDOUT with the current request id as a default log tag.
  config.log_tags = [ :request_id ]
  config.logger   = ActiveSupport::TaggedLogging.logger(STDOUT)

  # Change to "debug" to log everything (including potentially personally-identifiable information!).
  config.log_level = ENV.fetch("RAILS_LOG_LEVEL", "info")

  # Prevent health checks from clogging up the logs.
  config.silence_healthcheck_path = "/up"

  # Don't log any deprecations.
  config.active_support.report_deprecations = false

  # Replace the default in-process memory cache store with a durable alternative.
  config.cache_store = :solid_cache_store

  # Replace the default in-process and non-durable queuing backend for Active Job.
  config.active_job.queue_adapter = :solid_queue
  config.solid_queue.connects_to = { database: { writing: :queue } }

  # Ignore bad email addresses and do not raise email delivery errors.
  # Set this to true and configure the email server for immediate delivery to raise delivery errors.
  # config.action_mailer.raise_delivery_errors = false

  # Set host to be used by links generated in mailer templates.
  config.action_mailer.default_url_options = { host: "example.com" }

  # Specify outgoing SMTP server. Remember to add smtp/* credentials via bin/rails credentials:edit.
  # config.action_mailer.smtp_settings = {
  #   user_name: Rails.application.credentials.dig(:smtp, :user_name),
  #   password: Rails.application.credentials.dig(:smtp, :password),
  #   address: "smtp.example.com",
  #   port: 587,
  #   authentication: :plain
  # }

  # Enable locale fallbacks for I18n (makes lookups for any locale fall back to
  # the I18n.default_locale when a translation cannot be found).
  config.i18n.fallbacks = true

  # Do not dump schema after migrations.
  config.active_record.dump_schema_after_migration = false

  # Only use :id for inspections in production.
  config.active_record.attributes_for_inspect = [ :id ]

  # Enable DNS rebinding protection and other `Host` header attacks.
  # config.hosts = [
  #   "example.com",     # Allow requests from example.com
  #   /.*\.example\.com/ # Allow requests from subdomains like `www.example.com`
  # ]
  #
  # Skip DNS rebinding protection for the default health check endpoint.
  # config.host_authorization = { exclude: ->(request) { request.path == "/up" } }
end

content.text: Now the plan document, reflecting Render free tier + auto-reseed (no disk) and English/LTR.

input.content:
# Implementation Plan — Jgive Donation Page Clone

Reference: https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-targets/159183 (client-rendered SPA — its
markup couldn't be scraped directly, so the structure below is inferred from the assignment brief
plus typical crowdfunding-page conventions; a visual pass against the live page happens in Phase 4).

## Decisions locked in

- **Deploy target:** Render, building directly from the repo's existing `Dockerfile` (no Kamal).
- **Database:** SQLite, no persistent disk. Render's free instance type has an ephemeral
  filesystem (disks require a paid plan — confirmed in Render's docs), so `bin/docker-entrypoint`
  runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed` on every boot. The app is always populated on load; the trade-off
  is that a donation submitted in one session won't survive the free service spinning down and
  restarting — acceptable since cross-session persistence isn't a stated requirement, and
  upgrading to a paid plan + real disk later is a one-line `render.yaml` change if needed.
- **Locale:** English / LTR. Mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and sticky-form structure —
  not its language or RTL direction. Fastest path within the time-box; called out explicitly so
  it's a documented trade-off, not an oversight.
- **Styling:** `tailwindcss-rails` gem — keeps the app Node-free (uses a standalone Tailwind
  binary), consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup.

## Phase 1 — Project Initialization & Configuration

1. Keep the Rails 8.1 skeleton as-is (Propshaft, importmap, Turbo/Stimulus, SQLite, Solid
   Queue/Cache/Cable already in `Gemfile`).
2. Add `tailwindcss-rails` for styling speed/fidelity.
3. Deliberately skip: Active Storage, ActionText, `friendly_id`, Faker/FactoryBot (rationale in
   Phase 2/4).
4. Remove or note-as-unused `.kamal/` and `config/deploy.yml` — dead weight once deploying via
   Render's native Docker build instead of Kamal.
5. `bundle install`, run `bin/rubocop -A` once for a clean omakase baseline.
6. Commit per phase/sub-step — real history is an explicit deliverable, not a single squash.

## Phase 2 — Database Schema & Setup (Migrations + Seeds)

**`Campaign`**
- `title:string`, `summary:string` (one-liner for cards/header), `story:text` (rendered with
  `simple_format`, no ActionText since there's no rich-text upload UI to justify it),
  `cover_image_url:string`, `goal_amount:decimal, precision: 10, scale: 2`.
- No `slug` — numeric ID routes (`/campaigns/159183`-style) match the reference URL's own pattern.
- No `raised_amount` column. `raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` / `donations_count` are
  instance methods derived from the `donations` association — at this scale, deriving from the
  source of truth is simpler than keeping a denormalized counter in sync.

**`Donation`**
- `campaign:references`, `amount:decimal, precision: 10, scale: 2`,
  `frequency:integer` enum (`one_time`/`monthly`, default `one_time`),
  `display_preference:integer` enum (`full_name`/`first_name_only`/`anonymous`, default `full_name`),
  `status:integer` enum (`pending`/`paid`, default `pending`),
  `donor_name:string`, `dedication_message:text` (optional).
- `donor_name` is always required, even when `display_preference: anonymous` — anonymity controls
  what's shown publicly, not what's collected internally.
- A `Donation.countable` scope (`pending` + `paid`) is what `Campaign#raised_amount` sums. The
  assignment wants progress to move when a *pending* donation is created (no payment flow exists
  to ever produce a `paid` one yet), so pending counts for now. Isolating that policy in one named
  scope makes "only `paid` counts" a one-line change once a real payment provider lands.

**Seeds (`db/seeds.rb`)**
- 2 campaigns, hand-written (not Faker) — deterministic and readable in a diff for ~2 records.
- Cover images bundled under `app/assets/images/seeds/`, referenced by filename via `image_tag` —
  no Active Storage, no external hotlinked URL, fully self-contained for a reliable demo.
- 6–10 donations across both campaigns, varied `frequency`/`display_preference`/
  `dedication_message` (some blank), mostly `pending`, one or two `paid` to prove the model
  supports it.
- Written idempotently (`find_or_create_by!`) since it now runs on every container boot
  (see Phase 5).

## Phase 3 — Controller Logic & Routing

```ruby
root to: "campaigns#index"
resources :campaigns, only: [:index, :show] do
  resources :donations, only: [:create]
end
```

- `CampaignsController#index` — minimal card grid, not the graded focus.
- `CampaignsController#show` — loads `@campaign`, a new unsaved `@donation` for the form, and
  `@campaign.donations.countable.recent` for the Donors tab.
- `DonationsController#create` — builds under `@campaign`; strong params on `:amount, :frequency,
  :display_preference, :donor_name, :dedication_message`.
  - `turbo_stream`: replace the progress bar, prepend to the donor list, swap the form for a
    thank-you state (or re-render the form with errors, `422`, on failure).
  - `html`: redirect to the campaign with a flash notice (standard Turbo fallback).
- Custom-amount handling stays server-naive: "preset vs. custom" merge is client-side JS (Phase 4),
  so the controller only ever sees one `donation[amount]` value.
- If time allows: a model validation test + one request spec for donation creation. If cut, name
  it explicitly in the README rather than dropping it silently.

## Phase 4 — Frontend (Grid, Hotwire Tabs, Sticky Form)

- Layout: light header, `<main>` yield, Tailwind via `tailwindcss-rails`.
- Show page: hero (cover image, title, summary) → stats bar (raised/goal via a reusable
  `_progress_bar` partial shared with index cards, donor count) → responsive grid
  (`grid-cols-1 lg:grid-cols-3`): left 2 cols = tabs, right 1 col = donation form.
- Tabs ("About", "Story", "Donors") — small Stimulus `tabs_controller.js` toggling a `hidden`
  class + `aria-selected` on server-rendered panels. No Turbo Frames needed; content is already in
  the DOM, this is pure client-side show/hide.
- Sticky form — plain CSS `position: sticky; top: …` on the right-column wrapper, no JS.
- Donation form:
  - Amount: preset radios + a separate "other amount" input. A tiny Stimulus
    `amount_controller.js` makes typing into "other" activate it as the selected radio, so exactly
    one `donation[amount]` is ever submitted.
  - Frequency toggle (one-time/monthly), display-preference radios, donor name, optional
    dedication textarea, submit.
- Checkpoint before closing this phase: eyeball the live reference page against ours for
  spacing/colors/section order, and note any deliberate deltas in the README's "what I changed and
  why" section per the brief's own invitation.

## Phase 5 — Deployment Strategy & README

**Deployment (Render, free tier, SQLite, no disk)**

1. `render.yaml` (added) — `runtime: docker`, `plan: free`, `dockerfilePath: ./Dockerfile`,
   `healthCheckPath: /up`, `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` as a dashboard secret (`sync: false`).
2. `bin/docker-entrypoint` (updated) — runs `db:prepare` then `db:seed` on every server boot, so
   the ephemeral free-tier filesystem never comes up blank.
3. Push to a public GitHub repo, connect Render via Blueprint (`render.yaml`), paste
   `config/master.key`'s contents into the `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` dashboard field, deploy.
4. Smoke-test the live URL: index → show → tab switching → submit a donation → progress bar and
   donor list update in place.

**README outline**
- Live URL + repo link.
- Run locally: `bin/setup`, `bin/rails db:seed`, `bin/dev`.
- Key decisions & trade-offs: derived `raised_amount` vs. counter cache; pending donations
  counting toward progress (`Donation.countable` scope as the seam for later); no Active
  Storage/admin (bundled seed images, no upload UI in scope); Tailwind for Node-free speed; free
  Render tier + reseed-on-boot instead of a paid disk; English/LTR for time-budget reasons.
- **Wiring a real payment provider:** Donation created `pending` → redirect to a provider-hosted
  checkout (Stripe Checkout, or an Israeli processor like Tranzila/PayMe — jgive itself loads
  `cdn.payme.io`) with the donation id in metadata → webhook verifies signature, flips status to
  `paid`, stores the provider's transaction id under a unique index for idempotency →
  failure/cancel webhook handled separately → redirect user back to a thank-you state. Only `paid`
  would count toward `Donation.countable` at that point.
- What I'd do with more time: test coverage, Hebrew/RTL + i18n, real image uploads + a minimal
  admin, recurring-billing automation, donor receipts, accessibility pass, a paid Render plan +
  persistent disk for real cross-session durability.
- AI usage note + pointer to the transcript file(s).

content.thinking:
The user wants me to reconsider adding a slug field to Campaign since it's straightforward to implement. I'm thinking through the simplest approach — either using the `friendly_id` gem or just adding a basic `slug:string` column and handling the slug generation manually, which would be faster than pulling in another dependency.

I'll go with the manual approach: add a migration for the slug column with a unique index, use a `before_validation` callback to generate the slug from the title via `parameterize`, override `to_param` to return the slug for URL generation, and update the controller to look up campaigns by slug instead of ID. This is genuinely simple — no gem needed, just a few lines of Rails conventions. I need to update the plan to reflect this decision in the schema and controller phases.

# Implementation Plan — Jgive Donation Page Clone

## Decisions locked in

- **Deploy target:** Render, building directly from the repo's existing `Dockerfile` (no Kamal).

  runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed` on every boot. The app is always populated on load; the trade-off
  is that a donation submitted in one session won't survive the free service spinning down and
  restarting — acceptable since cross-session persistence isn't a stated requirement, and
  upgrading to a paid plan + real disk later is a one-line `render.yaml` change if needed.
- **Locale:** English / LTR. Mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and sticky-form structure —
  not its language or RTL direction. Fastest path within the time-box; called out explicitly so
  it's a documented trade-off, not an oversight.
- **Styling:** `tailwindcss-rails` gem — keeps the app Node-free (uses a standalone Tailwind
  binary), consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup.
- **URLs:** campaigns get a readable slug (`/campaigns/help-build-a-community-center`), via a
  plain `slug` column + `to_param` override — no `friendly_id` gem. Simple enough not to need the
  dependency: one migration column, one `before_validation` callback, one `find_by!` in the
  controller.

Created three files reflecting Render + free tier + slug:

- **[render.yaml](render.yaml)** — Blueprint for Render's Docker runtime, free plan, `/up` health check, `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` as a dashboard secret. No disk block.
- **[bin/docker-entrypoint](bin/docker-entrypoint)** — now runs `db:seed` after `db:prepare` on every boot, since the free tier's ephemeral filesystem means SQLite resets on restart; this keeps the page from ever loading empty.
- **[PLAN.md](PLAN.md)** — the 5-phase roadmap, updated with the Render-free-tier-no-disk decision and a manual `slug`/`to_param` approach for readable campaign URLs (skipping the `friendly_id` gem since it's a one-column, one-callback addition).

Ready to start on Phase 1 whenever you want — just say go.
phase 1 - ask me to alloy before you performed

content.text:
Phase 1 has a few discrete actions. Per your preference, I'll confirm each one before running it rather than doing them all at once. Here's the breakdown:

1. Add `tailwindcss-rails` to the `Gemfile`, run `bundle install`, then run the Tailwind install generator (sets up `app/assets/tailwind/application.css` and wires it into the layout).
2. Decide what to do with `.kamal/` and `config/deploy.yml` (delete them, since we're deploying via Render's Docker build, not Kamal).
3. Run `bin/rubocop -A` once for a clean baseline.
4. Commit the result.

Want me to start with **step 1** (add the gem + bundle install + Tailwind generator)?
yes

file.content:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
  <head>
    <title><%= content_for(:title) || "Jgive Assignment" %></title>
    <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width,initial-scale=1">
    <meta name="apple-mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes">
    <meta name="application-name" content="Jgive Assignment">
    <meta name="mobile-web-app-capable" content="yes">
    <%= csrf_meta_tags %>
    <%= csp_meta_tag %>

    <%= yield :head %>

    <%# Enable PWA manifest for installable apps (make sure to enable in config/routes.rb too!) %>
    <%#= tag.link rel: "manifest", href: pwa_manifest_path(format: :json) %>

    <link rel="icon" href="/icon.png" type="image/png">
    <link rel="icon" href="/icon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">
    <link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/icon.png">

    <%# Includes all stylesheet files in app/assets/stylesheets %>
    <%= stylesheet_link_tag :app, "data-turbo-track": "reload" %>
    <%= javascript_importmap_tags %>
  </head>

  <body>
    <main class="container mx-auto mt-28 px-5 flex">
      <%= yield %>
    </main>
  </body>
</html>

I'm realizing that `stylesheet_link_tag :app` was already present before, pulling in all stylesheets from the Propshaft bundle rather than Tailwind's specific compiled output. With tailwindcss-rails and Propshaft together, the generator typically adds a separate stylesheet link pointing to the compiled Tailwind build at app/assets/builds.

Let me verify whether Propshaft's `:app` bundle automatically includes app/assets/builds in its load paths (which is standard in Rails 8 with Propshaft), and check the actual compiled output filename and asset configuration to understand how everything's being bundled together.

file.content:
source "https://rubygems.org"

# Bundle edge Rails instead: gem "rails", github: "rails/rails", branch: "main"
gem "rails", "~> 8.1.3"
# The modern asset pipeline for Rails [https://github.com/rails/propshaft]
gem "propshaft"
# Tailwind CSS, via a standalone executable (no Node required) [https://github.com/rails/tailwindcss-rails]
gem "tailwindcss-rails"
# Use sqlite3 as the database for Active Record
gem "sqlite3", ">= 2.1"
# Use the Puma web server [https://github.com/puma/puma]
gem "puma", ">= 5.0"
# Use JavaScript with ESM import maps [https://github.com/rails/importmap-rails]
gem "importmap-rails"
# Hotwire's SPA-like page accelerator [https://turbo.hotwired.dev]
gem "turbo-rails"
# Hotwire's modest JavaScript framework [https://stimulus.hotwired.dev]
gem "stimulus-rails"
# Build JSON APIs with ease [https://github.com/rails/jbuilder]
gem "jbuilder"

# Use Active Model has_secure_password [https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_model_basics.html#securepassword]
# gem "bcrypt", "~> 3.1.7"

# Windows does not include zoneinfo files, so bundle the tzinfo-data gem
gem "tzinfo-data", platforms: %i[ windows jruby ]

# Use the database-backed adapters for Rails.cache, Active Job, and Action Cable
gem "solid_cache"
gem "solid_queue"
gem "solid_cable"

# Reduces boot times through caching; required in config/boot.rb
gem "bootsnap", require: false

# Deploy this application anywhere as a Docker container [https://kamal-deploy.org]
gem "kamal", require: false

# Add HTTP asset caching/compression and X-Sendfile acceleration to Puma [https://github.com/basecamp/thruster/]
gem "thruster", require: false

# Use Active Storage variants [https://guides.rubyonrails.org/active_storage_overview.html#transforming-images]
gem "image_processing", "~> 1.2"

group :development, :test do
  # See https://guides.rubyonrails.org/debugging_rails_applications.html#debugging-with-the-debug-gem
  gem "debug", platforms: %i[ mri windows ], require: "debug/prelude"

  # Audits gems for known security defects (use config/bundler-audit.yml to ignore issues)
  gem "bundler-audit", require: false

  # Static analysis for security vulnerabilities [https://brakemanscanner.org/]
  gem "brakeman", require: false

  # Omakase Ruby styling [https://github.com/rails/rubocop-rails-omakase/]
  gem "rubocop-rails-omakase", require: false
end

group :development do
  # Use console on exceptions pages [https://github.com/rails/web-console]
  gem "web-console"
end

group :test do
  # Use system testing [https://guides.rubyonrails.org/testing.html#system-testing]
  gem "capybara"
  gem "selenium-webdriver"
end

content.thinking:
I'll start with the Donation model, setting up a belongs_to association with Campaign and an enum for frequency that defaults to one_time. I'm thinking through the enum validation options available in Rails 7+ — the validate option might not be built-in by default, so I may need to add a custom validation for the frequency field.

Actually, since the database column has a NOT NULL DEFAULT constraint and enums raise an ArgumentError on invalid assignment, I don't need explicit validation for the enum itself. Now I'm defining the Donation model with three enums (frequency, display_preference, and status), presence and numericality validations for the donor fields, a countable scope that filters for pending and paid donations, a recent scope for ordering, and a display_name method that returns the donor name based on their display preference setting.

Writing the Donation model...

Now moving to the Campaign model with associations, slug generation, validations for title, slug uniqueness, and goal amount, plus a to_param method for routing and a raised_amount method that's being defined.

Finishing the Campaign model with methods to calculate progress percentage, count donations, and auto-generate slugs from the title.

input.old_string:
class Donation < ApplicationRecord
  belongs_to :campaign
