input.content:
# Jgive Campaign Donation Page

A Rails reproduction of a Jgive campaign donation page (reference: [jgive.com/.../donation-targets/159183](https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-targets/159183)) — campaign details, tabs, and a donation form that creates a `pending` donation and updates the campaign's progress. No payment/checkout, no accounts/admin, per the assignment scope.

**Live app:** _TODO: paste the Render URL here once deployed_
**Repo:** https://github.com/Yael-Peretz/jgive-campaign-donation

## Running locally

Requires Ruby 3.3.1 (see `.ruby-version`) and SQLite — no Node/Postgres needed.

```bash
bin/setup        # bundle install, prepare the db
bin/rails db:seed
bin/dev          # rails server + tailwind --watch
```

Visit `http://localhost:3000`.

## Key decisions & trade-offs

- **`raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` are derived, not stored.** `Campaign` has no
  `raised_amount` column — both are computed from the `donations` association on read. At this
  scale, deriving from the source of truth is simpler than keeping a denormalized counter in sync,
  and it can't drift out of sync with the actual donation rows.
- **Pending donations count toward progress.** The assignment explicitly wants progress to move
  when a donation is submitted, but there's no payment flow to ever produce a `paid` one. A
  `Donation.countable` scope (currently `pending` + `paid`) is what gets summed, so narrowing it to
  `where(status: :paid)` later is a one-line change, not a hunt through the codebase.
- **Donor name is required unless the donor chooses to stay anonymous.** Anonymity means skipping
  the name entirely, not just hiding an internally-collected one.
- **No Active Storage, no admin.** Campaigns are seed-only (no creation/upload UI is in scope), so
  cover images are plain SVGs checked into `app/assets/images/seeds/` and referenced by filename —
  no blob storage, no external image host to go down mid-demo.
- **Readable campaign URLs via a plain `slug` column + `to_param` override**, not the `friendly_id`
  gem — one column, one callback, one `find_by!` didn't justify the dependency.
- **Tailwind via `tailwindcss-rails`** (standalone binary), not a Node toolchain — keeps the app
  consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup from `rails new`.
- **SQLite on Render's free tier, no persistent disk.** Render's free instances have an ephemeral
  filesystem (disks require a paid plan), so `bin/docker-entrypoint` runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed`
  on every boot — the app is never blank, at the cost of donations not surviving a spin-down/restart.
  Documented in [PLAN.md](PLAN.md); upgrading to a paid plan + a real disk is a one-line
  `render.yaml` change if that trade-off stops being acceptable.
- **English/LTR**, not Hebrew/RTL. The build mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and donation
  flow structurally, but not its language or text direction — the fastest path within the time-box.
- **Donation form is a popup, not an always-visible inline form.** The reference site opens
  donation as a modal (see `.../donate/amount`); this build uses the native `<dialog>` element +
  a small Stimulus controller rather than a hand-rolled modal, so focus-trapping, Esc-to-close, and
  the backdrop come from the browser for free.

## What I changed from the original design, and why

- **Collapsed the reference's multi-step donation flow (amount → details → payment) into one
  single-step modal.** Since payment/checkout is explicitly out of scope, a wizard with no final
  step to wizard towards added complexity without adding value — one form covering amount,
  frequency, display preference, name, and dedication captures everything this build actually does.
- **Tab structure (About / Story / Donors) is inferred, not copied.** The live reference is a
  client-rendered SPA that returns an empty shell to both `curl` and the WebFetch tool, so its
  actual tab markup/labels were never directly observable. The three tabs here were chosen to
  match the assignment brief's own field list (title, description/story, progress) plus a donor
  list, organized the way most crowdfunding pages structure this content. Worth a manual
  side-by-side glance against the live page if pixel-level fidelity matters more than structural
  fidelity.

## Wiring a real payment provider

Today, submitting the form creates a `Donation` with `status: pending` directly — no payment
happens. To make it real:

1. On `DonationsController#create`, after saving the `pending` donation, redirect to a
   provider-hosted checkout (Stripe Checkout, or an Israeli processor like Tranzila/PayMe — the
   reference site itself loads `cdn.payme.io`) with the donation's id in the session/metadata.
2. The provider redirects back on success/cancel; either way, the donation stays `pending` until
   confirmed — never trust the redirect alone.
3. Add a webhook endpoint that verifies the provider's signature, looks up the donation by the id
   in the metadata, and flips it to `paid`. Store the provider's transaction/session id under a
   unique index so a retried webhook can't double-process the same payment.
4. Narrow `Donation.countable` (in `app/models/donation.rb`) from `[:pending, :paid]` to just
   `:paid`, so progress only reflects confirmed money.
5. Handle the failure/cancel webhook by moving the donation to a new `failed` enum value rather
   than leaving it `pending` forever.

## What I'd do with more time

- Test coverage — model validations, a request spec for donation creation (happy path + the
  anonymous-no-name case), a system test for tab switching and the donation modal.
- Hebrew/RTL + i18n, to actually match the reference's language and direction.
- Real image uploads (Active Storage) and a minimal admin, so campaigns aren't seed-only.
- Recurring-billing automation for `monthly` donations once a payment provider is wired in.
- Donor email receipts.
- An accessibility pass (the modal and tabs use semantic roles/`aria-selected`, but haven't been
  tested with a screen reader).
- A paid Render plan + persistent disk, for donations to survive a restart.

## AI usage

Built with Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6) end-to-end — planning, implementation, and debugging. The full
transcript is included with this submission alongside this README.

Now I need to figure out the best way forward with deployment—since I can't directly access Render or push code for the user, I should ask whether they already have a Render account and guide them through connecting it via the Blueprint, or offer an alternative approach if they prefer.

I also need to be careful about the README changes: following their established workflow, I shouldn't commit these myself—they should review and commit. For validating the Dockerfile and render.yaml without Docker available locally, I'll check the YAML syntax using Ruby's built-in parser and verify the Dockerfile is Rails' standard generated version, plus confirm the entrypoint script is sound since we've already tested those database commands directly.

content.text:
README is written. Since deploying requires your Render account/dashboard (no `gh`/`docker` CLI available in this environment to do it for you), here's what's left and who does what:

**Ready on my side:**
- `render.yaml` — validated as syntactically correct YAML.
- `bin/docker-entrypoint` — runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed` on boot.
- `README.md` — full rewrite (run-locally steps, key decisions, payment-wiring plan, what I'd do with more time). Not committed yet — want me to commit it, or will you?

**Needs you (Render dashboard):**
1. Render → New → Blueprint → connect the `Yael-Peretz/jgive-campaign-donation` repo (already pushed, confirmed up to date with `origin/main`). Render should auto-detect `render.yaml`.
2. When it prompts for `RAILS_MASTER_KEY`, paste the contents of your local `config/master.key`.
3. Deploy.
4. Send me the resulting URL and I'll smoke-test it the same way I tested locally (index → show → tabs → donate → progress update), and we'll paste it into the README's `TODO` line.

Want me to commit the README now, or hold off until you've reviewed it?

add to the readme and do not commit yet
## Payment Provider Integration (Future Scope)

Currently, the application records donations in a `pending` state and immediately updates the campaign's progress. In a production environment, updating the campaign progress would only occur after successful payment verification. 

To wire in a real payment provider (e.g., Stripe, Tranzila, or CreditGuard), I would implement the following architecture:

1. **Checkout Initialization:** Upon form submission, the backend creates a `Donation` record with `status: pending`. It then calls the payment provider's API to generate a secure Checkout Session or Payment Intent, storing the provider's reference ID on the donation record.

2. **Client Handoff:** The user is redirected to the provider's hosted checkout page (or an embedded secure iframe) to complete the transaction securely, ensuring the app doesn't touch raw credit card data (PCI compliance).

3. **Asynchronous Webhooks (The Source of Truth):** State transitions should never rely on the user's browser redirecting back to a "Success URL", as the browser might crash or be closed. Instead, the system will expose a webhook endpoint (e.g., `/webhooks/payments`).

4. **Webhook Processing & State Transition:**
   * **Verification:** The endpoint will first verify the webhook signature using the provider's secret to ensure authenticity.
   * **Async Processing:** To prevent timeouts, the endpoint immediately returns a `200 OK` and enqueues a background job (e.g., via Sidekiq / ActiveJob).
   * **State Machine:** The background worker retrieves the `Donation`, and safely transitions its state from `pending` to `paid` (ideally using a state machine like the `aasm` gem). 
   * **Side Effects:** Only upon transitioning to `paid`, the system will increment the campaign's `amount_raised` (using pessimistic locking or database atomic counters like `increment_counter` to avoid race conditions during high-traffic campaigns), generate a tax receipt, and trigger a confirmation email.

5. **Idempotency & Reliability:** To prevent double-counting donations in case the provider sends the same webhook event twice, the processing logic will be idempotent, tracking processed event IDs. Failed payments will transition the donation state to `failed`, allowing the user to seamlessly retry.

1	# Jgive Campaign Donation Page
2	
3	A Rails reproduction of a Jgive campaign donation page (reference: [jgive.com/.../donation-targets/159183](https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-targets/159183)) — campaign details, tabs, and a donation form that creates a `pending` donation and updates the campaign's progress. No payment/checkout, no accounts/admin, per the assignment scope.
4	
5	**Live app:** _TODO: paste the Render URL here once deployed_
6	**Repo:** https://github.com/Yael-Peretz/jgive-campaign-donation
7	
8	## Running locally
9	
10	Requires Ruby 3.3.1 (see `.ruby-version`) and SQLite — no Node/Postgres needed.
11	
12	```bash
13	bin/setup        # bundle install, prepare the db
14	bin/rails db:seed
15	bin/dev          # rails server + tailwind --watch
16	```
17	
18	Visit `http://localhost:3000`.
19	
20	## Key decisions & trade-offs
21	
22	- **`raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` are derived, not stored.** `Campaign` has no
23	  `raised_amount` column — both are computed from the `donations` association on read. At this
24	  scale, deriving from the source of truth is simpler than keeping a denormalized counter in sync,
25	  and it can't drift out of sync with the actual donation rows.
26	- **Pending donations count toward progress.** The assignment explicitly wants progress to move
27	  when a donation is submitted, but there's no payment flow to ever produce a `paid` one. A
28	  `Donation.countable` scope (currently `pending` + `paid`) is what gets summed, so narrowing it to
29	  `where(status: :paid)` later is a one-line change, not a hunt through the codebase.
30	- **Donor name is required unless the donor chooses to stay anonymous.** Anonymity means skipping
31	  the name entirely, not just hiding an internally-collected one.
32	- **No Active Storage, no admin.** Campaigns are seed-only (no creation/upload UI is in scope), so
33	  cover images are plain SVGs checked into `app/assets/images/seeds/` and referenced by filename —
34	  no blob storage, no external image host to go down mid-demo.
35	- **Readable campaign URLs via a plain `slug` column + `to_param` override**, not the `friendly_id`
36	  gem — one column, one callback, one `find_by!` didn't justify the dependency.
37	- **Tailwind via `tailwindcss-rails`** (standalone binary), not a Node toolchain — keeps the app
38	  consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup from `rails new`.
39	- **SQLite on Render's free tier, no persistent disk.** Render's free instances have an ephemeral
40	  filesystem (disks require a paid plan), so `bin/docker-entrypoint` runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed`
41	  on every boot — the app is never blank, at the cost of donations not surviving a spin-down/restart.
42	  Documented in [PLAN.md](PLAN.md); upgrading to a paid plan + a real disk is a one-line
43	  `render.yaml` change if that trade-off stops being acceptable.
44	- **English/LTR**, not Hebrew/RTL. The build mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and donation
45	  flow structurally, but not its language or text direction — the fastest path within the time-box.
46	- **Donation form is a popup, not an always-visible inline form.** The reference site opens
47	  donation as a modal (see `.../donate/amount`); this build uses the native `<dialog>` element +
48	  a small Stimulus controller rather than a hand-rolled modal, so focus-trapping, Esc-to-close, and
49	  the backdrop come from the browser for free.
50	
51	## What I changed from the original design, and why
52	
53	- **Collapsed the reference's multi-step donation flow (amount → details → payment) into one
54	  single-step modal.** Since payment/checkout is explicitly out of scope, a wizard with no final
55	  step to wizard towards added complexity without adding value — one form covering amount,
56	  frequency, display preference, name, and dedication captures everything this build actually does.
57	- **Tab structure (About / Story / Donors) is inferred, not copied.** The live reference is a
58	  client-rendered SPA that returns an empty shell to both `curl` and the WebFetch tool, so its
59	  actual tab markup/labels were never directly observable. The three tabs here were chosen to
60	  match the assignment brief's own field list (title, description/story, progress) plus a donor
61	  list, organized the way most crowdfunding pages structure this content. Worth a manual
62	  side-by-side glance against the live page if pixel-level fidelity matters more than structural
63	  fidelity.
64	
65	## Wiring a real payment provider
66	
67	Today, submitting the form creates a `Donation` with `status: pending` directly — no payment
68	happens. To make it real:
69	
70	1. On `DonationsController#create`, after saving the `pending` donation, redirect to a
71	   provider-hosted checkout (Stripe Checkout, or an Israeli processor like Tranzila/PayMe — the
72	   reference site itself loads `cdn.payme.io`) with the donation's id in the session/metadata.
73	2. The provider redirects back on success/cancel; either way, the donation stays `pending` until
74	   confirmed — never trust the redirect alone.
75	3. Add a webhook endpoint that verifies the provider's signature, looks up the donation by the id
76	   in the metadata, and flips it to `paid`. Store the provider's transaction/session id under a
77	   unique index so a retried webhook can't double-process the same payment.
78	4. Narrow `Donation.countable` (in `app/models/donation.rb`) from `[:pending, :paid]` to just
79	   `:paid`, so progress only reflects confirmed money.
80	5. Handle the failure/cancel webhook by moving the donation to a new `failed` enum value rather
81	   than leaving it `pending` forever.
82	
83	## What I'd do with more time
84	
85	- Test coverage — model validations, a request spec for donation creation (happy path + the
86	  anonymous-no-name case), a system test for tab switching and the donation modal.
87	- Hebrew/RTL + i18n, to actually match the reference's language and direction.
88	- Real image uploads (Active Storage) and a minimal admin, so campaigns aren't seed-only.
89	- Recurring-billing automation for `monthly` donations once a payment provider is wired in.
90	- Donor email receipts.
91	- An accessibility pass (the modal and tabs use semantic roles/`aria-selected`, but haven't been
92	  tested with a screen reader).
93	- A paid Render plan + persistent disk, for donations to survive a restart.
94	
95	## AI usage
96	
97	Built with Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6) end-to-end — planning, implementation, and debugging. The full
98	transcript is included with this submission alongside this README.
99

file.content:
# Jgive Campaign Donation Page

A Rails reproduction of a Jgive campaign donation page (reference: [jgive.com/.../donation-targets/159183](https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-targets/159183)) — campaign details, tabs, and a donation form that creates a `pending` donation and updates the campaign's progress. No payment/checkout, no accounts/admin, per the assignment scope.

**Live app:** _TODO: paste the Render URL here once deployed_
**Repo:** https://github.com/Yael-Peretz/jgive-campaign-donation

## Running locally

Requires Ruby 3.3.1 (see `.ruby-version`) and SQLite — no Node/Postgres needed.

```bash
bin/setup        # bundle install, prepare the db
bin/rails db:seed
bin/dev          # rails server + tailwind --watch
```

Visit `http://localhost:3000`.

## Key decisions & trade-offs

- **`raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` are derived, not stored.** `Campaign` has no
  `raised_amount` column — both are computed from the `donations` association on read. At this
  scale, deriving from the source of truth is simpler than keeping a denormalized counter in sync,
  and it can't drift out of sync with the actual donation rows.
- **Pending donations count toward progress.** The assignment explicitly wants progress to move
  when a donation is submitted, but there's no payment flow to ever produce a `paid` one. A
  `Donation.countable` scope (currently `pending` + `paid`) is what gets summed, so narrowing it to
  `where(status: :paid)` later is a one-line change, not a hunt through the codebase.
- **Donor name is required unless the donor chooses to stay anonymous.** Anonymity means skipping
  the name entirely, not just hiding an internally-collected one.
- **No Active Storage, no admin.** Campaigns are seed-only (no creation/upload UI is in scope), so
  cover images are plain SVGs checked into `app/assets/images/seeds/` and referenced by filename —
  no blob storage, no external image host to go down mid-demo.
- **Readable campaign URLs via a plain `slug` column + `to_param` override**, not the `friendly_id`
  gem — one column, one callback, one `find_by!` didn't justify the dependency.
- **Tailwind via `tailwindcss-rails`** (standalone binary), not a Node toolchain — keeps the app
  consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup from `rails new`.
- **SQLite on Render's free tier, no persistent disk.** Render's free instances have an ephemeral
  filesystem (disks require a paid plan), so `bin/docker-entrypoint` runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed`
  on every boot — the app is never blank, at the cost of donations not surviving a spin-down/restart.
  Documented in [PLAN.md](PLAN.md); upgrading to a paid plan + a real disk is a one-line
  `render.yaml` change if that trade-off stops being acceptable.
- **English/LTR**, not Hebrew/RTL. The build mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and donation
  flow structurally, but not its language or text direction — the fastest path within the time-box.
- **Donation form is a popup, not an always-visible inline form.** The reference site opens
  donation as a modal (see `.../donate/amount`); this build uses the native `<dialog>` element +
  a small Stimulus controller rather than a hand-rolled modal, so focus-trapping, Esc-to-close, and
  the backdrop come from the browser for free.

## What I changed from the original design, and why

- **Collapsed the reference's multi-step donation flow (amount → details → payment) into one
  single-step modal.** Since payment/checkout is explicitly out of scope, a wizard with no final
  step to wizard towards added complexity without adding value — one form covering amount,
  frequency, display preference, name, and dedication captures everything this build actually does.
- **Tab structure (About / Story / Donors) is inferred, not copied.** The live reference is a
  client-rendered SPA that returns an empty shell to both `curl` and the WebFetch tool, so its
  actual tab markup/labels were never directly observable. The three tabs here were chosen to
  match the assignment brief's own field list (title, description/story, progress) plus a donor
  list, organized the way most crowdfunding pages structure this content. Worth a manual
  side-by-side glance against the live page if pixel-level fidelity matters more than structural
  fidelity.

## Wiring a real payment provider

Today, submitting the form creates a `Donation` with `status: pending` directly — no payment
happens. To make it real:

1. On `DonationsController#create`, after saving the `pending` donation, redirect to a
   provider-hosted checkout (Stripe Checkout, or an Israeli processor like Tranzila/PayMe — the
   reference site itself loads `cdn.payme.io`) with the donation's id in the session/metadata.
2. The provider redirects back on success/cancel; either way, the donation stays `pending` until
   confirmed — never trust the redirect alone.
3. Add a webhook endpoint that verifies the provider's signature, looks up the donation by the id
   in the metadata, and flips it to `paid`. Store the provider's transaction/session id under a
   unique index so a retried webhook can't double-process the same payment.
4. Narrow `Donation.countable` (in `app/models/donation.rb`) from `[:pending, :paid]` to just
   `:paid`, so progress only reflects confirmed money.
5. Handle the failure/cancel webhook by moving the donation to a new `failed` enum value rather
   than leaving it `pending` forever.

## What I'd do with more time

- Test coverage — model validations, a request spec for donation creation (happy path + the
  anonymous-no-name case), a system test for tab switching and the donation modal.
- Hebrew/RTL + i18n, to actually match the reference's language and direction.
- Real image uploads (Active Storage) and a minimal admin, so campaigns aren't seed-only.
- Recurring-billing automation for `monthly` donations once a payment provider is wired in.
- Donor email receipts.
- An accessibility pass (the modal and tabs use semantic roles/`aria-selected`, but haven't been
  tested with a screen reader).
- A paid Render plan + persistent disk, for donations to survive a restart.

## AI usage

Built with Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6) end-to-end — planning, implementation, and debugging. The full
transcript is included with this submission alongside this README.

input.new_string:
## Payment Provider Integration (Future Scope)

Currently, the application records donations in a `pending` state and immediately updates the
campaign's progress. In a production environment, updating the campaign progress would only occur
after successful payment verification.

To wire in a real payment provider (e.g., Stripe, Tranzila, or CreditGuard), I would implement the
following architecture:

1. **Checkout Initialization:** Upon form submission, the backend creates a `Donation` record with
   `status: pending`. It then calls the payment provider's API to generate a secure Checkout
   Session or Payment Intent, storing the provider's reference ID on the donation record.

2. **Client Handoff:** The user is redirected to the provider's hosted checkout page (or an
   embedded secure iframe) to complete the transaction securely, ensuring the app doesn't touch
   raw credit card data (PCI compliance).

3. **Asynchronous Webhooks (The Source of Truth):** State transitions should never rely on the
   user's browser redirecting back to a "Success URL", as the browser might crash or be closed.
   Instead, the system will expose a webhook endpoint (e.g., `/webhooks/payments`).

4. **Webhook Processing & State Transition:**
   * **Verification:** The endpoint will first verify the webhook signature using the provider's
     secret to ensure authenticity.
   * **Async Processing:** To prevent timeouts, the endpoint immediately returns a `200 OK` and
     enqueues a background job (e.g., via Sidekiq / ActiveJob).
   * **State Machine:** The background worker retrieves the `Donation`, and safely transitions its
     state from `pending` to `paid` (ideally using a state machine like the `aasm` gem).
   * **Side Effects:** Only upon transitioning to `paid`, the system will increment the campaign's
     `amount_raised` (using pessimistic locking or database atomic counters like
     `increment_counter` to avoid race conditions during high-traffic campaigns), generate a tax
     receipt, and trigger a confirmation email.

5. **Idempotency & Reliability:** To prevent double-counting donations in case the provider sends
   the same webhook event twice, the processing logic will be idempotent, tracking processed event
   IDs. Failed payments will transition the donation state to `failed`, allowing the user to
   seamlessly retry.

1	# Jgive Campaign Donation Page
2	
3	A Rails reproduction of a Jgive campaign donation page (reference: [jgive.com/.../donation-targets/159183](https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-targets/159183)) — campaign details, tabs, and a donation form that creates a `pending` donation and updates the campaign's progress. No payment/checkout, no accounts/admin, per the assignment scope.
4	
5	**Live app:** _TODO: paste the Render URL here once deployed_
6	**Repo:** https://github.com/Yael-Peretz/jgive-campaign-donation
7	
8	## Running locally
9	
10	Requires Ruby 3.3.1 (see `.ruby-version`) and SQLite — no Node/Postgres needed.
11	
12	```bash
13	bin/setup        # bundle install, prepare the db
14	bin/rails db:seed
15	bin/dev          # rails server + tailwind --watch
16	```
17	
18	Visit `http://localhost:3000`.
19	
20	## Key decisions & trade-offs
21	
22	- **`raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` are derived, not stored.** `Campaign` has no
23	  `raised_amount` column — both are computed from the `donations` association on read. At this
24	  scale, deriving from the source of truth is simpler than keeping a denormalized counter in sync,
25	  and it can't drift out of sync with the actual donation rows.
26	- **Pending donations count toward progress.** The assignment explicitly wants progress to move
27	  when a donation is submitted, but there's no payment flow to ever produce a `paid` one. A
28	  `Donation.countable` scope (currently `pending` + `paid`) is what gets summed, so narrowing it to
29	  `where(status: :paid)` later is a one-line change, not a hunt through the codebase.
30	- **Donor name is required unless the donor chooses to stay anonymous.** Anonymity means skipping
31	  the name entirely, not just hiding an internally-collected one.
32	- **No Active Storage, no admin.** Campaigns are seed-only (no creation/upload UI is in scope), so
33	  cover images are plain SVGs checked into `app/assets/images/seeds/` and referenced by filename —
34	  no blob storage, no external image host to go down mid-demo.
35	- **Readable campaign URLs via a plain `slug` column + `to_param` override**, not the `friendly_id`
36	  gem — one column, one callback, one `find_by!` didn't justify the dependency.
37	- **Tailwind via `tailwindcss-rails`** (standalone binary), not a Node toolchain — keeps the app
38	  consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup from `rails new`.
39	- **SQLite on Render's free tier, no persistent disk.** Render's free instances have an ephemeral
40	  filesystem (disks require a paid plan), so `bin/docker-entrypoint` runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed`
41	  on every boot — the app is never blank, at the cost of donations not surviving a spin-down/restart.
42	  Documented in [PLAN.md](PLAN.md); upgrading to a paid plan + a real disk is a one-line
43	  `render.yaml` change if that trade-off stops being acceptable.
44	- **English/LTR**, not Hebrew/RTL. The build mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and donation
45	  flow structurally, but not its language or text direction — the fastest path within the time-box.
46	- **Donation form is a popup, not an always-visible inline form.** The reference site opens
47	  donation as a modal (see `.../donate/amount`); this build uses the native `<dialog>` element +
48	  a small Stimulus controller rather than a hand-rolled modal, so focus-trapping, Esc-to-close, and
49	  the backdrop come from the browser for free.
50	
51	## What I changed from the original design, and why
52	
53	- **Collapsed the reference's multi-step donation flow (amount → details → payment) into one
54	  single-step modal.** Since payment/checkout is explicitly out of scope, a wizard with no final
55	  step to wizard towards added complexity without adding value — one form covering amount,
56	  frequency, display preference, name, and dedication captures everything this build actually does.
57	- **Tab structure (About / Story / Donors) is inferred, not copied.** The live reference is a
58	  client-rendered SPA that returns an empty shell to both `curl` and the WebFetch tool, so its
59	  actual tab markup/labels were never directly observable. The three tabs here were chosen to
60	  match the assignment brief's own field list (title, description/story, progress) plus a donor
61	  list, organized the way most crowdfunding pages structure this content. Worth a manual
62	  side-by-side glance against the live page if pixel-level fidelity matters more than structural
63	  fidelity.
64	
65	## Payment Provider Integration (Future Scope)
66	
67	Currently, the application records donations in a `pending` state and immediately updates the
68	campaign's progress. In a production environment, updating the campaign progress would only occur
69	after successful payment verification.
70	
71	To wire in a real payment provider (e.g., Stripe, Tranzila, or CreditGuard), I would implement the
72	following architecture:
73	
74	1. **Checkout Initialization:** Upon form submission, the backend creates a `Donation` record with
75	   `status: pending`. It then calls the payment provider's API to generate a secure Checkout
76	   Session or Payment Intent, storing the provider's reference ID on the donation record.
77	
78	2. **Client Handoff:** The user is redirected to the provider's hosted checkout page (or an
79	   embedded secure iframe) to complete the transaction securely, ensuring the app doesn't touch
80	   raw credit card data (PCI compliance).
81	
82	3. **Asynchronous Webhooks (The Source of Truth):** State transitions should never rely on the
83	   user's browser redirecting back to a "Success URL", as the browser might crash or be closed.
84	   Instead, the system will expose a webhook endpoint (e.g., `/webhooks/payments`).
85	
86	4. **Webhook Processing & State Transition:**
87	   * **Verification:** The endpoint will first verify the webhook signature using the provider's
88	     secret to ensure authenticity.
89	   * **Async Processing:** To prevent timeouts, the endpoint immediately returns a `200 OK` and
90	     enqueues a background job (e.g., via Sidekiq / ActiveJob).
91	   * **State Machine:** The background worker retrieves the `Donation`, and safely transitions its
92	     state from `pending` to `paid` (ideally using a state machine like the `aasm` gem).
93	   * **Side Effects:** Only upon transitioning to `paid`, the system will increment the campaign's
94	     `amount_raised` (using pessimistic locking or database atomic counters like
95	     `increment_counter` to avoid race conditions during high-traffic campaigns), generate a tax
96	     receipt, and trigger a confirmation email.
97	
98	5. **Idempotency & Reliability:** To prevent double-counting donations in case the provider sends
99	   the same webhook event twice, the processing logic will be idempotent, tracking processed event
100	   IDs. Failed payments will transition the donation state to `failed`, allowing the user to
101	   seamlessly retry.
102	
103	## What I'd do with more time
104	
105	- Test coverage — model validations, a request spec for donation creation (happy path + the
106	  anonymous-no-name case), a system test for tab switching and the donation modal.
107	- Hebrew/RTL + i18n, to actually match the reference's language and direction.
108	- Real image uploads (Active Storage) and a minimal admin, so campaigns aren't seed-only.
109	- Recurring-billing automation for `monthly` donations once a payment provider is wired in.
110	- Donor email receipts.
111	- An accessibility pass (the modal and tabs use semantic roles/`aria-selected`, but haven't been
112	  tested with a screen reader).
113	- A paid Render plan + persistent disk, for donations to survive a restart.
114	
115	## AI usage
116	
117	Built with Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6) end-to-end — planning, implementation, and debugging. The full
118	transcript is included with this submission alongside this README.

file.content:
# Jgive Campaign Donation Page

A Rails reproduction of a Jgive campaign donation page (reference: [jgive.com/.../donation-targets/159183](https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-targets/159183)) — campaign details, tabs, and a donation form that creates a `pending` donation and updates the campaign's progress. No payment/checkout, no accounts/admin, per the assignment scope.

**Live app:** _TODO: paste the Render URL here once deployed_
**Repo:** https://github.com/Yael-Peretz/jgive-campaign-donation

## Running locally

Requires Ruby 3.3.1 (see `.ruby-version`) and SQLite — no Node/Postgres needed.

```bash
bin/setup        # bundle install, prepare the db
bin/rails db:seed
bin/dev          # rails server + tailwind --watch
```

Visit `http://localhost:3000`.

## Key decisions & trade-offs

- **`raised_amount` / `progress_percentage` are derived, not stored.** `Campaign` has no
  `raised_amount` column — both are computed from the `donations` association on read. At this
  scale, deriving from the source of truth is simpler than keeping a denormalized counter in sync,
  and it can't drift out of sync with the actual donation rows.
- **Pending donations count toward progress.** The assignment explicitly wants progress to move
  when a donation is submitted, but there's no payment flow to ever produce a `paid` one. A
  `Donation.countable` scope (currently `pending` + `paid`) is what gets summed, so narrowing it to
  `where(status: :paid)` later is a one-line change, not a hunt through the codebase.
- **Donor name is required unless the donor chooses to stay anonymous.** Anonymity means skipping
  the name entirely, not just hiding an internally-collected one.
- **No Active Storage, no admin.** Campaigns are seed-only (no creation/upload UI is in scope), so
  cover images are plain SVGs checked into `app/assets/images/seeds/` and referenced by filename —
  no blob storage, no external image host to go down mid-demo.
- **Readable campaign URLs via a plain `slug` column + `to_param` override**, not the `friendly_id`
  gem — one column, one callback, one `find_by!` didn't justify the dependency.
- **Tailwind via `tailwindcss-rails`** (standalone binary), not a Node toolchain — keeps the app
  consistent with the existing importmap/Propshaft setup from `rails new`.
- **SQLite on Render's free tier, no persistent disk.** Render's free instances have an ephemeral
  filesystem (disks require a paid plan), so `bin/docker-entrypoint` runs `db:prepare` + `db:seed`
  on every boot — the app is never blank, at the cost of donations not surviving a spin-down/restart.
  Documented in [PLAN.md](PLAN.md); upgrading to a paid plan + a real disk is a one-line
  `render.yaml` change if that trade-off stops being acceptable.
- **English/LTR**, not Hebrew/RTL. The build mirrors the reference's sections, tabs, and donation
  flow structurally, but not its language or text direction — the fastest path within the time-box.
- **Donation form is a popup, not an always-visible inline form.** The reference site opens
  donation as a modal (see `.../donate/amount`); this build uses the native `<dialog>` element +
  a small Stimulus controller rather than a hand-rolled modal, so focus-trapping, Esc-to-close, and
  the backdrop come from the browser for free.

## What I changed from the original design, and why

- **Collapsed the reference's multi-step donation flow (amount → details → payment) into one
  single-step modal.** Since payment/checkout is explicitly out of scope, a wizard with no final
  step to wizard towards added complexity without adding value — one form covering amount,
  frequency, display preference, name, and dedication captures everything this build actually does.
- **Tab structure (About / Story / Donors) is inferred, not copied.** The live reference is a
  client-rendered SPA that returns an empty shell to both `curl` and the WebFetch tool, so its
  actual tab markup/labels were never directly observable. The three tabs here were chosen to
  match the assignment brief's own field list (title, description/story, progress) plus a donor
  list, organized the way most crowdfunding pages structure this content. Worth a manual
  side-by-side glance against the live page if pixel-level fidelity matters more than structural
  fidelity.

## Payment Provider Integration (Future Scope)

Currently, the application records donations in a `pending` state and immediately updates the
campaign's progress. In a production environment, updating the campaign progress would only occur
after successful payment verification.

To wire in a real payment provider (e.g., Stripe, Tranzila, or CreditGuard), I would implement the
following architecture:

1. **Checkout Initialization:** Upon form submission, the backend creates a `Donation` record with
   `status: pending`. It then calls the payment provider's API to generate a secure Checkout
   Session or Payment Intent, storing the provider's reference ID on the donation record.

2. **Client Handoff:** The user is redirected to the provider's hosted checkout page (or an
   embedded secure iframe) to complete the transaction securely, ensuring the app doesn't touch
   raw credit card data (PCI compliance).

3. **Asynchronous Webhooks (The Source of Truth):** State transitions should never rely on the
   user's browser redirecting back to a "Success URL", as the browser might crash or be closed.
   Instead, the system will expose a webhook endpoint (e.g., `/webhooks/payments`).

4. **Webhook Processing & State Transition:**
   * **Verification:** The endpoint will first verify the webhook signature using the provider's
     secret to ensure authenticity.
   * **Async Processing:** To prevent timeouts, the endpoint immediately returns a `200 OK` and
     enqueues a background job (e.g., via Sidekiq / ActiveJob).
   * **State Machine:** The background worker retrieves the `Donation`, and safely transitions its
     state from `pending` to `paid` (ideally using a state machine like the `aasm` gem).
   * **Side Effects:** Only upon transitioning to `paid`, the system will increment the campaign's
     `amount_raised` (using pessimistic locking or database atomic counters like
     `increment_counter` to avoid race conditions during high-traffic campaigns), generate a tax
     receipt, and trigger a confirmation email.

5. **Idempotency & Reliability:** To prevent double-counting donations in case the provider sends
   the same webhook event twice, the processing logic will be idempotent, tracking processed event
   IDs. Failed payments will transition the donation state to `failed`, allowing the user to
   seamlessly retry.

## What I'd do with more time

- Test coverage — model validations, a request spec for donation creation (happy path + the
  anonymous-no-name case), a system test for tab switching and the donation modal.
- Hebrew/RTL + i18n, to actually match the reference's language and direction.
- Real image uploads (Active Storage) and a minimal admin, so campaigns aren't seed-only.
- Recurring-billing automation for `monthly` donations once a payment provider is wired in.
- Donor email receipts.
- An accessibility pass (the modal and tabs use semantic roles/`aria-selected`, but haven't been
  tested with a screen reader).
- A paid Render plan + persistent disk, for donations to survive a restart.

## AI usage

Built with Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6) end-to-end — planning, implementation, and debugging. The full
transcript is included with this submission alongside this README.

     receipt, and trigger a confirmation email.

5. **Idempotency & Reliability:** To prevent double-counting donations in case the provider sends
   the same webhook event twice, the processing logic will be idempotent, tracking processed event
   IDs. Failed payments will transition the donation state to `failed`, allowing the user to
   seamlessly retry.

## What I'd do with more time

- Test coverage — model validations, a request spec for donation creation (happy path + the
  anonymous-no-name case), a system test for tab switching and the donation modal.
- Hebrew/RTL + i18n, to actually match the reference's language and direction.
- Real image uploads (Active Storage) and a minimal admin, so campaigns aren't seed-only.
- Recurring-billing automation for `monthly` donations once a payment provider is wired in.
- Donor email receipts.
- An accessibility pass (the modal and tabs use semantic roles/`aria-selected`, but haven't been
  tested with a screen reader).
- A paid Render plan + persistent disk, for donations to survive a restart.

## AI usage

Built with Claude Code (Sonnet 4.6) end-to-end — planning, implementation, and debugging. The full
transcript is included with this submission alongside this README.

No payment/checkout, no accounts/admin, per the assignment scope.

# Chat Transcript — Jgive Campaign Donation Page Assignment

Exported from the Claude Code session log (JSONL). Internal extended-thinking blocks are omitted; large tool inputs/outputs are truncated for readability — the actual file contents are visible in the repo and git history instead of being duplicated here in full.

---

### User

Backend Developer (Ruby on Rails) — Home Assignment
Overview
Build a small Rails app that reproduces a specific Jgive campaign donation page — the part a
donor sees and interacts with, up to (but not including) payment.

Your reference is this live campaign: https://www.jgive.com/new/he/ils/donation-
targets/159183

Aim to get the result close to the existing design — it doesn't need to be pixel-perfect, but it
should clearly resemble the original. If something in the current design strikes you as off, feel
free to improve it — just tell us what you changed and why.
Time
Aim for roughly 4–6 hours. We care far more about your judgment than about completeness. If
you run short, cut scope deliberately and tell us what you cut and why. Please don't polish
endlessly — a focused, well-reasoned submission beats a sprawling one.
What to build (core)
A page for the campaign above that shows:
Campaign details, organized into tabs (mirroring the tabs on the reference page): title,
description / story, cover image, goal amount, amount raised, and progress toward the goal.
A donation form: amount (a few preset options + a custom amount), one-time vs. recurring,
donor display preference (full name / first name only / anonymous), and an optional
dedication message.
Submitting the form should create a donation record in a pending state and update the
campaign's progress. Do not implement payment / checkout. Instead, in your README, briefly
describe how you'd wire in a real payment provider and move a donation from pending to
paid .
Seed the app with one or two campaigns and a handful of donations so the page isn't empty on
first load.

Explicitly out of scope
Payment processing / checkout
User accounts, login, or admin
Production hardening

Deliverables
1. A public Git repo (or send us an invite). We'd love to see real commit history rather than a
single squashed commit.
2. A deployed, live application (mandatory). Deploy the app and include a link where we can
use it.
3. A README covering: how to run it locally, the key decisions and trade-offs you made, and
what you'd do with more time.
4. The full chat transcript(s) with whatever LLM / coding assistant you used.
5. A few sentences on your setup and thought process — your tools, how you approached
the problem, and where AI helped or got in your way.

On using AI
Use whatever tools you normally use. We use AI heavily here, and we want to see how you work
with it — not whether you avoided it. Sharing your transcript helps us understand how you steer,
review, and decide what to keep. You should also be ready to walk us through your code and
extend it together in the next interview.
Submitting
Send us the repo link, the live URL, and your transcript and notes. Questions are very welcome at
any point — just reach out.

Please output a structured step-by-step implementation plan optimized for speed and clean Rails conventions. 

Break the plan down into these phases:
Project Initialization & Configurations 
Database Schema & Setup (Migrations and Seeds)
Controller Logic & Routing
Frontend View Layout (Main page grid, JS/Hotwire Tabs, Sticky Form)
Deployment Strategy & README Outline

Do not write any application code yet. Just provide the step-by-step roadmap so we can align on the architecture first.

---

### Assistant

I'll first check the current repo skeleton and look at the reference campaign page to ground the plan in actual specifics, then produce the phased roadmap without writing code.

---

content.thinking: The output looks clean and faithful to the conversation, so I'm satisfied with the quality. Now I should verify the file size is reasonable and scan for any sensitive content leaks, particularly around that RAILS_MASTER_KEY discussion, before wrapping up.

input.command: grep -n -i "master.key\|master_key" /tmp/transcript_export/TRANSCRIPT.md | head -20
input.description: Sanity check that no secret values leaked into the exported transcript

content.content:
552:6	# docker run -d -p 80:80 -e RAILS_MASTER_KEY=<value from config/master.key> --name jgive_assignment jgive_assignment
654:2. Add a `render.yaml` blueprint: web service, `env: docker`, free plan, one disk mounted at `/rails/storage` (1GB), `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` as a dashboard secret, `RAILS_ENV=production`. Gives reviewers a reproducible deploy config, not just a dashboard click-through.
655:3. Push to a public GitHub repo, connect Render (Blueprint or manual Web Service), set the master key secret, deploy.
977:  "content": "# Render Blueprint — see https://render.com/docs/blueprint-spec
#
# Deploys straight from the repo's existing Dockerfile. No persistent disk:
# the free instance type's filesystem is ephemeral by design (Render docs:
# disks require a paid plan), so SQLite resets on every restart/redeploy.
# bin/docker-entrypoint runs db:prepare + db:seed on every boot to make that
# a non-issue — the app always comes up populated, never blank.
services:
  - type: web
    name: jgive-assignment
    runtime: docker
    plan: free
    dockerfilePath: ./Dockerfile
    dockerContext: .
    healthCheckPath: /up
    envVars:
      - key: RAILS_MASTER_KEY
        sync: false # paste the contents of config/master.key in the Render dashboard
      - key: RAILS_ENV
        value: production
      - key: RAILS_LOG_TO_STDOUT
        value: "true"
"
1157:- **[render.yaml](render.yaml)** — Blueprint for Render's Docker runtime, free plan, `/up` health check, `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` as a dashboard secret. No disk block.
6766:      - key: RAILS_MASTER_KEY
6767:        sync: false # paste the contents of config/master.key in the Render dashboard
6924:2. When it prompts for `RAILS_MASTER_KEY`, paste the contents of your local `config/master.key`.
7071:what should be RAILS_MASTER_KEY?
7079:The value is just the contents of your local `config/master.key` file. Get it and paste it **directly into Render's dashboard field**, not here:
7089:  "command": "git check-ignore -v config/master.key 2>&1; echo "---"; git log --all --oneline -- config/master.key",
7090:  "description": "Confirm master.key is gitignored and was never committed"
7101:.gitignore:34:/config/*.key	config/master.key
7109:Good — confirmed `config/master.key` is gitignored and has never been committed (no history for it). On your machine, run:
7112:cat config/master.key
7115:Copy that single line and paste it directly into Render's `RAILS_MASTER_KEY` field in the dashboard. That's the only place it should go.

lastPrompt: export a transcript all of this chat

content.thinking:
The secret references are safely abstracted as placeholders with no actual values exposed. Now I'm planning to move the exported files into the repo—copying the transcript markdown to the root and including the raw JSONL data for completeness.

Since the assignment explicitly asks for "the full chat transcript," I think providing both the readable markdown version and the raw JSONL backing data best satisfies that requirement. They're easy to remove later if needed, so I'll add both files to the working tree and let them decide what to keep. I should also quickly verify the `.gitignore` won't silently exclude these files, and consider whether to use uppercase naming like `TRANSCRIPT.md` and `TRANSCRIPT.jsonl` to match the existing convention.

git -C /home/yael/jgive-assignment check-ignore -v TRANSCRIPT.md transcript.jsonl 2>&1 || echo "not ignored"
ls -la /home/yael/jgive-assignment/TRANSCRIPT.md /home/yael/jgive-assignment/transcript.jsonl
